love.shortReport:
The premier three-down back in this class. Love combines explosive burst with a developing receiving skill set that gives him legitimate weekly RB1 upside in PPR formats. His tape reveals a runner who wins with suddenness — he reaches top speed in two strides and punishes defenses that over-pursue the front side of zone concepts. The receiving growth from afterthought to legitimate weapon is the trait that separates him from the pure runners in this class.

What makes Love different from the other elite backs in this draft is the trajectory of his pass-catching. In 2023 he caught eight balls on check-downs and screens. By 2025 he's running angle routes, option routes out of the slot, and even some wheel concepts — the kind of route tree that offensive coordinators draw up for players they want to feature in the passing game. That development curve is steep enough to project 50+ catches within two NFL seasons if he lands with a creative coordinator.

The fantasy profile is straightforward: Love is a three-down back with RB1 weekly upside in PPR and RB2 floor in standard. The 17-touchdown season in 2024 proves he can score near the goal line, the 27 catches in 2025 prove he can contribute in the passing game, and the return ability gives him a hidden floor booster that most running backs don't have. He's the safest elite RB in this class and the one with the clearest path to weekly RB1 production.
love.athleticProfile: Love's movement profile is defined by his first-step explosiveness and the rare ability to maintain top speed through the hole rather than decelerating at contact. His hips are loose enough to make the corner on outside-zone runs without losing forward lean — a trait that translates directly to the NFL's most common run concept. He doesn't have the lateral agility of a Jahmyr Gibbs or the pure long speed of a Breece Hall, but his burst-to-top-speed transition is among the best in this class. The contact balance shows up on every carry: he absorbs the first defender with his pad level and keeps his legs driving through the finish. His estimated 4.38 speed is evident on tape when he gets to the second level — he pulls away from defensive backs who have proper pursuit angles. The 34-inch vertical at his size suggests the explosiveness is real and measurable, not just tape intuition.
strengths: The burst is his signature trait. Watch any outside-zone clip and you'll see Love press the hole vertically, force the backside linebacker to over-commit, and then bounce the run with the kind of sudden redirection that makes the second defender take a false step. He doesn't dance — he commits and explodes. That decisiveness is what NFL offensive coordinators want in a zone-runner.

The receiving development is real and accelerating. In 2023 he was a check-down option with eight catches — by 2025 he's running actual route concepts (angle routes, swing screens, even a few option routes out of the slot) and catching 27 balls with two touchdowns. His hands are natural; he doesn't fight the ball or body-catch. The route-running is still rudimentary but the trajectory is steep. If he lands with a creative OC, he could be catching 50+ balls within two seasons.

His goal-line finishing is instinctive, not just powerful. He doesn't run through people like Derrick Henry — he reads the leverage of the defensive line, finds the soft shoulder, and squeezes through gaps that don't look open on the broadcast angle. Seventeen touchdowns in 2024 behind a Notre Dame offensive line that was average at best tells you he's creating his own scoring opportunities, not riding elite blocking.

The return ability is an underappreciated dimension of his floor. Two kick-return touchdowns in 2024 and a punt-return score in 2025 prove he's dangerous in space even when the play isn't designed for him. In fantasy, this is a hidden floor booster — even in weeks where the offense stalls, he can produce a return touchdown that swings your matchup.
weaknesses: Pass protection is his most glaring technical deficit. He leans into rushers with his shoulder instead of sitting into his stance and mirroring with his feet. This gets him washed by inside stunts and late-delayed blitzes. NFL coordinators will hesitate to leave him in on third-and-long until this improves, which caps his passing-down snaps and therefore his receiving volume in the short term.

He can be too patient on zone reads, particularly the split-zone and read-option concepts that Notre Dame ran heavily. There are clips where he reads the defensive end correctly but then hesitates on the pull, giving the scraping linebacker time to close the gap. In the NFL, that half-second of hesitation is the difference between a 12-yard gain and a 2-yard gain. He needs to trust his eyes and commit faster.

The declining yards-per-carry (7.9 to 6.9 to 6.6) is a legitimate concern when you dig into the context. In 2023 he had limited carries against defenses keying on Audric Estime. In 2024 he was the focal point and still produced. But in 2025, defenses loaded the box with single-high safety looks and dared Notre Dame to throw — and Love's efficiency dipped. He may need a QB who can keep defenses honest to maximize his rushing upside.

He's never carried the ball 200 times in a season. The durability question isn't about injury history (he's been remarkably healthy) but about unknown tolerance. Can he handle 280 carries plus 50 targets over a 17-game NFL season? We're projecting, not confirming.
love.nflFit:
Love is a schematic fit for any outside-zone system that wants a three-down back. His ideal landing spot is a Shanahan-style offense that uses zone runs with play-action bootlegs and features the running back in the passing game on designed routes, not just check-downs. He'd thrive in San Francisco, Miami, or Detroit — all teams that run wide-zone concepts and throw to their backs. He'd struggle in a gap-scheme power running game where he's asked to press the hole and power through a stacked box. He's not that kind of runner.

The specific scheme matters because Love's burst is maximized when he's running on the move rather than pressing a predetermined gap. Outside-zone and wide-zone concepts allow him to use his burst-to-top-speed transition — he presses the hole vertically, forces the backside linebacker to over-commit, and then bounces the run with sudden redirection. In a gap scheme, that burst is wasted because the hole is predetermined and the runner is supposed to hit it north-south regardless of what the defense does. Love needs the freedom to read and react, which is what zone concepts provide.

The passing-game role is equally important. Love isn't just a check-down receiver — he's developing into a legitimate route-runner who can win on angle routes, option routes, and wheel concepts. An offensive coordinator who designs plays specifically for Love in the passing game will unlock his three-down value. A coordinator who treats him as a dump-off option will waste his most differentiating trait.
love.comp:
The best modern comparison is Alvin Kamara's receiving arc with Dalvin Cook's rushing burst. Like Kamara, Love's receiving role is growing year over year and could become a defining feature of his game. Like Cook, he wins with burst and corner-press ability on outside-zone runs. He doesn't have Kamara's elusiveness in space or Cook's top-end long speed, but the combination of both profiles makes him a unique three-down weapon.

The Kamara comparison is specifically about the receiving trajectory, not the overall player profile. Kamara entered the league as a passing-down specialist and developed into a three-down workhorse. Love is on a similar arc — he entered college as a pure runner and is developing into a legitimate receiving threat. If the trajectory continues, Love could be catching 50-60 passes per season by his third NFL year, which would make him a top-5 dynasty RB regardless of his rushing volume.

The Cook comparison is about the rushing style — specifically the burst and corner-press ability on outside-zone runs. Like Cook, Love reaches top speed in two strides and punishes defenses that over-pursue. He doesn't have Cook's home-run speed, but he has the same decisiveness and the same ability to make the first defender miss with a subtle cut rather than a flashy juke.
love.dynastyOutlook:
Love is a top-3 dynasty RB in this class with a realistic path to weekly RB1 production in PPR formats. The key variable is landing spot — if he goes to a team with a zone-run identity and a creative passing-game coordinator, he's a locked-in three-down starter from Week 1. If he lands in a committee or a gap-scheme offense, his ceiling is capped at RB2. The receiving growth curve is the most important trend to monitor: if he continues on his current trajectory, he could be catching 50-60 passes by his second or third NFL season, which would make him a top-5 dynasty RB regardless of rushing volume.

The floor is remarkably high for a college running back. Even in a worst-case scenario — he lands in a committee on a gap-scheme team — Love's goal-line ability (17 TDs in 2024) and return value (2 KR TDs, 1 PR TD) give him standalone fantasy value that most committee backs lack. In PPR, his 27 catches in 2025 project to 35-40 catches as a rookie even in a limited role, which gives him an RB3 floor. That floor is higher than most running backs in this class.

The ceiling is a top-5 dynasty RB who produces 1,200+ rushing yards, 50+ catches, and 12+ touchdowns per season. That's the Alvin Kamara profile — a three-down weapon who never leaves the field and produces in every phase of the game. Love has the talent to reach that ceiling; the question is whether his landing spot and coordinator will give him the opportunity.
love.badgersFit:
Love is the kind of player you build a dynasty team around. The Badgers need a franchise running back and Love is the best one in this class — it's that simple. His three-down profile means he never leaves the field, which is critical in PPR leagues where every touch matters. The receiving upside isn't a bonus; it's the differentiator. A running back who catches 50+ balls and scores 12+ touchdowns is a top-5 fantasy asset. At pick 1.03, you're getting that ceiling at a discount because the RB position is so thin in dynasty. Don't overthink this — take Love and enjoy the positional advantage he gives you every week.

The specific value for the Badgers is the positional scarcity. In a 12-team dynasty league, there are maybe 8-10 running backs who produce consistent RB1 numbers. Love has a realistic chance to be one of them from Day 1. If you pass on him at 1.03, you're rolling the dice on finding a franchise RB later in the draft — and the hit rate on Day 2+ running backs is dramatically lower than on first-round talents. The floor is an RB2 with TD upside; the ceiling is a top-5 dynasty RB for the next half-decade.

The only scenario where you consider passing on Love is if you're absolutely set at RB and you need a quarterback or wide receiver more. But even then, Love's trade value at the RB position makes him worth drafting and flipping if necessary. He's the most liquid asset in this class — every dynasty team needs running backs, and Love is the best one available.
love.verdict: Elite RB1 profile with three-down upside. Draft and never look back.

mendoza.shortReport:
The QB1 of this class for fantasy purposes, and it's not particularly close. Mendoza's 2025 season at Indiana was a legitimate breakout — not a product of system or competition, but a genuine leap in processing speed, decision-making, and willingness to use his legs as a weapon. The arm talent has always been there; what changed at Indiana was his ability to marry that arm talent with efficient, on-schedule quarterback play. Add six rushing touchdowns and you have a dual-threat QB with a weekly fantasy floor that no pure pocket passer in this class can match.

The decision-making leap is what separates Mendoza from every other quarterback on this board. At Cal, he was a hero-ball gunslinger who trusted his arm to fit balls into windows that shouldn't be thrown. At Indiana, he became a processor — checking down when appropriate, throwing receivers open instead of waiting for them to be open, and playing the position with the kind of efficiency that NFL coordinators demand. The 35-6 TD-INT ratio isn't just a stat; it's evidence of a fundamental shift in how he reads the field.

The fantasy profile is anchored by the rushing floor. Six rushing touchdowns in 2025 means Mendoza can produce a QB1 line even in weeks where the passing game is merely average. In a bad passing week — 180 yards and one TD — he'll add 30 rushing yards and a goal-line score to salvage a QB2 floor. That's the kind of weekly safety net that wins dynasty championships, and it's something no pocket-only QB in this class can provide.
mendoza.athleticProfile: Mendoza is an above-average athlete who moves with purpose, not panic. The distinction matters: he's not a scrambler who runs when his first read is covered. He's a quarterback who climbs the pocket with his eyes downfield, buys time with subtle shifts in the pocket, and only takes off when the defense gives him a clear running lane. His rushing production climbed every year — from 112 yards at Cal to 198 to 312 at Indiana — not because he got faster, but because he got more comfortable identifying when to run versus when to extend and throw. That's a learned skill, not an athletic trait, and it's trending in the right direction. He's not going to run designed QB powers or read-options 10 times a game, but he'll convert third-and-4 with his legs three times a game, and those conversions add up to fantasy points that pure pocket passers leave on the table.
strengths: The arm talent is elite and it shows up on every type of throw. He can drive the ball 55 yards on a line with enough velocity to fit it into tight windows before the safety can rotate. But what separates him from other big-arm QBs is the touch layering — he can throw the same deep ball with different trajectories depending on the coverage. Against single-high, he'll drive it on a line to the boundary. Against two-high, he'll arc it over the corner and in front of the safety. That kind of ball placement isn't teachable at this level; it's instinctive.

The decision-making leap at Indiana is the single most important development in his profile. At Cal, Mendoza was a talented arm who made too many hero throws — forcing balls into coverage, extending plays only to throw into double coverage, trusting his arm to fit balls that shouldn't be thrown. At Indiana, something clicked. He went from a 17-6 and 16-6 TD-INT ratio to 35-6. That's not just better weapons — that's a fundamental shift in how he processes the field. He's checking down instead of forcing. He's throwing receivers open instead of waiting for them to be open. He's playing the position the way NFL coordinators need it played.

The off-schedule playmaking is where his fantasy ceiling lives. When the play breaks down — the pocket collapses, the coverage takes away all three reads, the clock in his head expires — Mendoza doesn't panic. He bails the pocket with his eyes still downfield, resets his platform on the move, and throws accurately to receivers who have improvised their routes. These are the plays that generate 40-yard touchdowns and 20-yard scrambles. In fantasy, these are the difference between a QB5 week and a QB1 week.

The quick release is an underappreciated trait that bails out offensive line deficiencies. He gets the ball out in 2.3 seconds on average, which is in the top quartet of college quarterbacks. That means he doesn't need an elite offensive line to function — he can produce behind a mediocre unit because the ball is gone before the rush arrives. This matters for fantasy because it means his production is less dependent on his NFL team's offensive line quality than most quarterbacks in this class.

The six rushing touchdowns in 2025 are the fantasy hook that separates him from every other QB in this class. In a bad passing week — say, 180 yards and one touchdown — Mendoza can still give you a QB1 line because he'll add 30 rushing yards and a goal-line touchdown. That floor is something Carson Beck, Drew Allar, and every pocket-only QB in this class simply cannot provide. In dynasty, where consistency matters more than ceiling, that floor is gold.
weaknesses: The hero-ball gene hasn't been fully eliminated — it's been suppressed. Watch the Indiana tape closely and you'll still see one or two throws per game that make you hold your breath. He'll try to fit a ball into a window that's closing, or he'll throw late over the middle because he trusts his arm too much. In the NFL, those are the throws that get intercepted by defensive backs who are faster and more experienced than college defenders. The 35-6 ratio is elite, but it's reasonable to expect regression toward 25-10 in the pros, which is still good but not elite.

His mechanics break down under pressure in a specific and concerning way. When the pocket collapses from the inside — a three-tech splitting the guard, a blitzing linebacker up the A-gap — Mendoza stops using his legs and becomes an arm-only thrower. He'll fade off his back foot and try to generate velocity with his upper body alone. Those throws flutter. They lose accuracy. They become interceptable. This is a fixable mechanical flaw — he needs to learn to climb the pocket and reset rather than retreating — but it's a flaw that will get exploited by NFL defensive coordinators who see it on tape.

The one-elite-season concern is real, even if the tape is convincing. At Cal, he was a 17-TD, 6-INT quarterback with modest efficiency. The leap to 35-6 at Indiana could represent genuine development, or it could represent a system and supporting cast that maximized his strengths and hid his weaknesses. The truth is probably somewhere in between. The risk is that the 2025 season is his ceiling, not his floor — and that the real Mendoza is closer to the Cal version than the Indiana version.

The completion percentage jump from 63.8% to 70.7% is suspicious on its face. Some of that is legitimate improvement in processing and accuracy. But some of it is Indiana's offense scheming easy completions — RPO pop passes, tunnel screens, and drag routes that pad the completion percentage without requiring elite accuracy. NFL defenses will take those away and force him to win from the pocket. Can he maintain that efficiency when the easy completions are gone?
mendoza.nflFit:
Mendoza needs a spread or RPO-based system that maximizes his arm talent and creates easy completions early in the progression. He's not a pure pocket quarterback — he needs bootleg and play-action elements that get him on the move, where he's at his best throwing on the run. The ideal offensive coordinator for Mendoza is someone like Shane Steffen or Kellen Moore — creative pass-game designers who use movement and misdirection to create open receivers. He'd struggle in a rigid West Coast system that demands seven-step drops and progression reads from a muddy pocket. The rushing touchdowns make him a fantasy QB1 regardless of system, but his passing ceiling depends heavily on the creativity of his coordinator.

The specific system fit matters because Mendoza's best throws come when he's moving — bootlegs, keepers, and rollouts where he can set his feet on the move and drive the ball downfield. His worst throws come when he's stationary in a collapsing pocket, where he tends to fade off his back foot and lose velocity. A system that features play-action and movement concepts will maximize his strengths and minimize his weaknesses. A system that asks him to stand in a muddy pocket and read progressions will expose his mechanical flaws.

The rushing touchdowns give him a fantasy floor that transcends system fit. Even in a bad offensive situation, Mendoza will produce 4-6 rushing touchdowns per season because he's willing to run near the goal line and he's effective at it. That floor makes him a QB1 in superflex and a QB2 in single-QB regardless of where he lands. But the passing ceiling — the difference between a QB5 and a QB1 — depends entirely on the coordinator and the offensive line.
mendoza.comp:
The trajectory comparison is Jordan Love — a raw, big-armed quarterback who needed reps and the right environment to unlock his potential. But the fantasy comparison is closer to a poor man's Jalen Hurts: a quarterback whose rushing touchdown floor gives him a weekly QB1 floor even when the passing is merely average. He doesn't have Hurts' designed-run volume or elite rushing ability, but six rushing touchdowns in a season is a legitimate fantasy floor that no pocket passer can match.

The Jordan Love comparison is about the developmental arc. Love sat behind Aaron Rodgers for three years, then exploded when he finally got the starting job. Mendoza's arc is similar — he was a raw talent at Cal who needed the right environment (Indiana) to unlock his potential. The risk is that the Cal version is closer to his true level; the upside is that the Indiana version is who he really is now.

The Jalen Hurts comparison is about the fantasy floor specifically. Hurts provides a weekly QB1 floor because he scores 10+ rushing touchdowns per season. Mendoza won't reach that volume, but 5-7 rushing TDs per year is enough to give him a similar floor advantage over pocket passers. In a week where he throws for 200 yards and one TD, the rushing contribution elevates him to a QB1 line. That's the Hurts-adjacent value proposition.
mendoza.dynastyOutlook:
Mendoza is the QB1 in this class for dynasty and it's not close. The combination of elite arm talent, a demonstrated decision-making leap, and rushing touchdown production gives him a three-dimensional fantasy profile that no other QB in this class can match. His ceiling is a top-3 fantasy QB — think Josh Allen without the 600-rush-yard floor. His floor is a top-12 fantasy QB — the rushing touchdowns alone guarantee that. In superflex leagues, he's worth a first-round pick. In single-QB leagues, he's worth a second-round pick as the quarterback who can win you a championship.

The key variable is whether the Indiana version is the real Mendoza or the Cal version. If the 35-6 TD-INT ratio and 70.7% completion represent genuine development, he's a top-5 fantasy QB within two seasons. If they represent a system peak, he's more of a QB8-12 with rushing floor. The truth is probably somewhere in between — expect 25-30 TDs, 8-10 INTs, and 5-6 rushing TDs as a rookie, which is a QB5-8 fantasy line with upside.

The rushing floor is what makes him draftable even in conservative scenarios. A quarterback who scores 5-6 rushing TDs per year provides a weekly floor that makes him startable in superflex regardless of his passing efficiency. That floor is his insurance policy — even if the passing development stalls, the rushing contribution keeps him on the QB1/QB2 borderline.
mendoza.badgersFit:
If you need a franchise quarterback, Mendoza is the pick and it's not complicated. The six rushing touchdowns give him a weekly floor that makes him startable even in bad matchups — something no pocket QB in this class can claim. In a dynasty league, a dual-threat quarterback with this arm talent is a foundational asset. He's your 1.01 if you're QB-needy, and he's worth a first-round pick as a trade asset even if you already have a quarterback. The only scenario where you pass on Mendoza is if you already have an elite QB and you're confident you can address the position later. Don't overthink it — the rushing floor is the separator.

The Badgers-specific value is the positional advantage. In superflex leagues, having a QB1 who produces 25+ passing TDs and 5+ rushing TDs is a championship-building advantage. Mendoza gives you that ceiling from Day 1. In single-QB, he's a streaming option with QB1 upside — the kind of player you draft in the 4th round and start when the matchup is right.

The risk is the one-year sample. If the Indiana version is the real Mendoza, you've got a top-5 dynasty QB at a discount. If the Cal version is closer to reality, you've got a QB2 with rushing floor. At pick 1.01, you're paying for the ceiling — and the rushing floor ensures that even the downside scenario is rosterable. That's the kind of risk-reward profile that wins dynasty leagues.
mendoza.verdict: High-end QB1 with elite ceiling. The rushing floor seals it.

tate.shortReport:
The prototypical X-receiver in this class — a big, physical boundary winner who dominates at the catch point and turns 50/50 balls into 80/20 propositions. What makes Tate special isn't just his size or his hands; it's the timing of his leap and his ability to track the ball over his shoulder while positioning his body between the defender and the throw. That's a skill that translates to any level because it's rooted in spatial awareness, not just athleticism. The 12-touchdown season in 2025 is the headline, but the real story is the development under Brian Hartline that transformed him from a raw deep-ball target into a complete route runner.

The contested-catch dominance is what makes him a fantasy asset from Day 1. Unlike receivers who need schemed touches or perfect throws to produce, Tate can catch the ball even when he's covered — because 'covered' for him doesn't mean the same thing it means for other receivers. He tracks the ball early, adjusts his body to create a window only he can access, and clamps the ball with genuine hand strength. That skill set is quarterback-proof to a degree, which means his fantasy floor is higher than most rookie receivers.

The risk is touchdown regression. Twelve TDs on 52 catches is a 23.1% rate that's nearly double the NFL average. Expect 7-9 TDs in a typical season, which is still excellent but changes his profile from 'elite TD scorer' to 'very good TD scorer with moderate volume.' In PPR, the 52-catch volume caps his floor. In standard, the TD upside makes him more valuable.
tate.athleticProfile: Tate is a size-speed-positioning winner, not a pure speed or suddenness winner. He doesn't have the explosive burst of a first-round wideout who separates with quick-twitch hip snap — instead, he creates separation through body positioning and leverage at the stem top. He'll lean into the corner to create a two-way go, then use his long arms to shield the ball from the defender's reach. His 22.2 yards per reception in 2025 tells you he's winning downfield, but it's not because he's running by people — it's because he's positioning his frame to make the catch while the defender is still trying to locate the ball. His leaping ability is functional rather than spectacular; he doesn't sky over defenders like Calvin Ridley, but he times his jump perfectly and catches the ball at the highest point of his reach, which is higher than most defenders can get to.
strengths: The contested-catch ability is his defining trait and it's rooted in three separate skills working in concert. First, he tracks the ball early and adjusts his body position to create a throwing window that only he can access. Second, he has genuine hand strength — he doesn't just catch the ball, he clamps it and refuses to let defenders rip it out even as they land on top of him. Third, he has an innate sense for when to leave his feet and when to stay grounded and box out, which means he's not wasting energy on unnecessary jumps. These three skills together make him a quarterback's best friend in the red zone — you can throw it to him even when he's covered, because 'covered' for Tate doesn't mean the same thing it means for other receivers.

The route tree development under Hartline is the most important improvement in his profile. In 2023, Tate was essentially a go-ball and fade specialist — he ran four routes and ran them well. By 2025, he's running the full tree: curl-stop, comeback, dig, post-dig, and even some option routes from the slot in four-receiver sets. The sharpness of his breaks isn't elite — he still rounds off some routes at the top — but the willingness and ability to run the full tree is what separates him from a one-trick deep-ball specialist. He's becoming a complete receiver, not just a jump-ball target.

The after-the-catch ability is an underappreciated dimension of his game because people assume a big receiver is a stiff receiver. Tate isn't that. He breaks arm tackles with his lean and keeps his legs driving through contact. His 22.2 YPR includes plenty of catch-and-run gains where he catches a 12-yard curl, sheds the corner, and turns it into a 25-yard gain. That's not a deep-ball stat — that's a yards-after-catch stat, and it tells you he's more than just a vertical threat.

The production trajectory is encouraging: 18 catches in 2023, 52 in 2024, 52 in 2025 with a massive touchdown spike. The volume plateaued but the efficiency exploded, which suggests he became a more effective player within the same role rather than just getting more opportunities. That's development, not just usage.
weaknesses: The separation question against elite press-man corners is the primary concern for his NFL projection. When a corner with quick-twitch hips and physical press ability gets hands on him at the line, Tate struggles to create the initial separation that makes his catch-point skills relevant. He can still win at the ball, but he's working harder to get there, and the quarterback has to make a more difficult throw. Against the Jaire Alexanders and Sauce Gardners of the world, he may be a non-factor on certain Sundays.

He doesn't have the top-end speed to stress defenses vertically on pure speed alone. His deep balls are won with positioning and tracking, not by running away from corners. That means he needs a quarterback who can layer the deep ball with touch and timing — a throw-it-up-and-let-him-go-get-it passer, not a fastball thrower. If his QB can't put the ball in the right spot, Tate's deep-ball value evaporates.

The touchdown rate is unsustainable and needs to be discussed honestly. Twelve touchdowns on 52 catches is a 23.1% rate, which is nearly double the NFL average for top receivers. Even the best red-zone receivers in the NFL settle around 12-15%. Expect regression to 7-9 touchdowns in a typical season, which is still excellent but changes his fantasy profile from 'elite TD scorer' to 'very good TD scorer with moderate volume.'

The slow start to his career (18 catches as a freshman) is a minor flag because it suggests he needed time to develop, which means he may need time at the NFL level too. He's not a plug-and-play producer like some rookie receivers — he may need a year of seasoning before he becomes the player his 2025 tape suggests he can be.
tate.nflFit:
Tate is an X-receiver who needs a vertical passing game and a quarterback who throws with anticipation and touch on 50/50 balls. He's not a system receiver — he's a quarterback-dependent receiver whose production scales with the quality of his passer. Put him with a top-10 QB and he's a WR1. Put him with a bottom-10 QB and he's a WR4 who occasionally wins a jump ball. The ideal fit is a team with an established franchise quarterback and a need for a boundary weapon — think Buffalo, Cincinnati, or the Chargers.

The quarterback dependency is the most important factor in his fantasy projection. Unlike a separator like Lemon who creates his own opportunities regardless of the throw, Tate needs the ball placed where only he can get it. A quarterback who throws with touch and timing on 50/50 balls will maximize Tate's catch-point skills. A quarterback who throws fastballs and expects receivers to be open will waste him.

The scheme fit is secondary to the quarterback fit, but it still matters. Tate thrives in offenses that use play-action to create single-high safety looks, which gives him one-on-one opportunities on the boundary. He struggles against two-high shells that force him to work underneath, where his route-running is still developing. An offense that takes deep shots off play-action is his ideal environment.
tate.comp:
The physical comparison is Mike Evans — same size, same catch-point dominance, same contested-catch reliance. But Tate is a step slower than Evans was coming out and doesn't have the same after-the-catch burst. The floor comparison is Mike Williams — a big, TD-dependent boundary receiver whose production fluctuates with his quarterback's ability to get him the ball in scoring position.

The Evans comparison is the ceiling. If Tate develops his route tree further and lands with an elite quarterback, he could produce Evans-like numbers: 70-80 catches, 1,100-1,300 yards, and 8-10 TDs per season. That's a WR1 in any format. The key difference is that Evans had rare deep speed for his size; Tate doesn't, which means he'll never produce the 20+ YPR seasons that Evans has.

The Williams comparison is the floor. Like Williams, Tate's production will fluctuate with his quarterback and his touchdown rate. In a good year with a good passer, he'll produce WR2 numbers. In a bad year with a bad passer, he'll produce WR4 numbers. The variance is built into his profile because he's a catch-point winner rather than a separator.
tate.dynastyOutlook:
Tate is a top-5 dynasty WR in this class with a WR2 floor and WR1 ceiling in the right situation. The touchdown dependency makes him boom/bust on a weekly basis — he'll win you weeks with two-TD games and disappear in weeks where the offense can't get him the ball near the end zone. In PPR, the moderate volume (52 catches) caps his floor. In standard scoring, the TD upside makes him more valuable. His long-term trajectory depends heavily on his NFL quarterback.

The weekly variance is the key concern for Tate managers. He'll give you 5-120-2 TD weeks that win matchups, then follow with 2-28-0 weeks that sink you. That boom/bust tendency is structural — it's a product of his catch-point profile, not a fluke. Receivers who win at the ball rather than creating separation naturally have more variance because their production depends on the quality of the throw and the game script.

The long-term trajectory depends on whether he develops his route tree enough to produce without relying on contested catches. If he becomes a more complete separator, the variance decreases and his floor rises. If he remains a catch-point specialist, the variance persists but the ceiling remains high. Either way, he's a top-5 dynasty WR in this class because the catch-point skill is rare and translatable.
tate.badgersFit:
Tate is a premium talent at a premium position and you should feel confident drafting him in the first round. The 12 touchdowns are flashy but expect 7-9 in a typical NFL season — that's still elite red-zone production. He's a WR2 with WR1 ceiling in PPR, and a borderline WR1 in standard scoring. At pick 1.04 he's a solid get if you went RB or QB earlier. The contested-catch skill is the key — it means he's quarterback-proof to a degree. Even with a mediocre passer, he'll produce because he doesn't need perfect throws to catch the ball. That's a rare and valuable trait in a rookie receiver.

The Badgers-specific value is the floor. Most rookie receivers are complete unknowns — they could be WR3s or they could be off the roster by October. Tate's catch-point skill gives him a rosterable floor even in a bad landing spot. He'll catch 50 balls and score 7 TDs as a floor, which is a WR3 in PPR and a WR2 in standard. That floor makes him a safer pick than most receivers in this class.

The ceiling depends on the quarterback he lands with. If he gets a top-10 passer, he's a WR1. If he gets a bottom-10 passer, he's a WR3 with TD upside. That variance is acceptable at pick 1.04 because the floor is high enough to justify the pick regardless of landing spot.
tate.verdict: Top-5 dynasty WR with contested-catch dominance. Plug and play with TD upside.

tyson.shortReport:
A vertical threat who overcame a 2023 knee injury to post back-to-back 1,000-yard seasons at Arizona State. Tyson's game is built on explosive speed and elite after-the-catch creativity — he's the kind of receiver who turns a 5-yard slant into a 60-yard touchdown because the first defender in space rarely makes the tackle. The knee injury is the risk that keeps him out of the top tier, but two full seasons of elite production post-injury suggest the explosiveness is real and sustainable. He's a WR2 with WR1 upside if the medicals check out.

The after-the-catch ability is what separates Tyson from other vertical threats in this class. Most deep-ball specialists are one-trick — they run fast in a straight line and that's it. Tyson can do that, but he's also devastating on schemed touches underneath. Give him the ball in space on a screen, slant, or crosser, and he'll make the first defender miss with a subtle hip-shift that creates a half-second of separation. That half-second is all he needs to accelerate past the second level. His 14.7 and 14.2 YPR aren't just deep-ball stats — they're catch-and-run stats.

The knee injury is the only thing keeping him out of the top tier. Two post-injury seasons of 1,000+ yards and double-digit touchdowns are the strongest possible signal that the explosiveness has returned, but NFL team doctors will scrutinize the surgical repair regardless. If the medicals check out, Tyson is a top-5 dynasty WR. If they don't, his draft capital could be suppressed and his rookie-year opportunity limited.
tyson.athleticProfile: Tyson is a twitchy, loose-hipped athlete whose movement profile is defined by suddenness rather than sustained top-end speed. His burst off the line is elite — he eats cushion against off-coverage and forces corners to open their hips before they want to. In breaks, his hips are loose enough to snap through direction changes without losing forward momentum, which is what makes him so dangerous after the catch. The 2023 knee injury was a legitimate concern, but the post-injury tape shows no loss of explosiveness — he's still pulling away from defensive backs at the second level and making defenders miss in space with the same suddenness he showed before the injury. His deep speed is real but it's more of a build-to-top-speed gear than an instant-jet quality; he doesn't hit his top gear as fast as a Tyreek Hill type, but once he's there, he maintains it through the finish. The punt-return ability (two returns over 50 yards in 2024) confirms that his short-area quickness and spatial awareness in space are genuine, not just a product of scheme.
strengths: The deep speed commands safety attention and opens up the entire offense. When Tyson lines up outside, defensive coordinators have to account for him with either a safety over the top or a corner with elite recovery speed. That constraint creates opportunities for the rest of the offense. But Tyson isn't just a decoy — he converts those deep targets at a high rate because he tracks the ball naturally over his shoulder and has the body control to adjust to underthrown balls without losing speed. That's a rare combination because most deep threats either run fast in a straight line or adjust to the ball well; Tyson does both.

The after-the-catch ability is his most translatable skill because it doesn't depend on scheme or quarterback quality. Give Tyson the ball in space — on a screen, a slant, a crosser, a quick out — and he'll make the first defender miss. His move is a subtle hip-shift that forces the defender to commit to one shoulder, then he bursts past the other side. It's not a juke or a stiff-arm; it's a body-feint rooted in hip looseness that creates a half-second of separation. That half-second is all he needs to accelerate past the second level. His 14.7 yards per reception in 2024 and 14.2 in 2025 aren't just deep-ball stats — they're catch-and-run stats that show he's generating yards after the catch on shorter routes.

The versatile alignment is a schematic advantage that NFL offensive coordinators will exploit. Tyson has lined up outside on both sides and in the slot at Arizona State, and he's produced from all three positions. Outside, he wins with speed and vertical routes. In the slot, he wins with quickness on option routes, angle routes, and crossers. That flexibility means he can be moved around formationally to create mismatches — put him in the slot against a slower nickel back, or motion him outside to force a safety rotation. The more places he can line up, the more ways the offense can get him the ball, and that translates directly to fantasy volume.

The post-injury production is the strongest possible signal that the knee is not a limiting factor. Two seasons of 1,000+ yards and double-digit touchdowns after an ACL-related injury is not a small sample — it's a sustained demonstration that the explosiveness has returned. The slight decline in yards per catch (14.7 to 14.2) could indicate a marginal loss of top-end speed, but it could also be explained by defensive adjustments or a change in target distribution. Either way, the production floor is established.
weaknesses: The knee injury history will follow him through the draft process regardless of his post-injury production. NFL team doctors will scrutinize the surgical repair, the rehabilitation timeline, and the structural integrity of the joint. Even if the tape shows no limitations, the medical evaluation could cause some teams to remove him from their board entirely or downgrade him significantly. In dynasty, this means his draft capital could be suppressed, which affects his rookie-year opportunity and therefore his fantasy production.

He rounds off routes at the top of the stem, particularly on deep comebacks and curl routes that require a hard plant-and-drive break. His hips are loose enough to make the turn, but he doesn't always sell the vertical stem before breaking, which allows corners to anticipate the route and jump the break point. This is a technique issue that can be coached, but it's a real limitation in the short term because it means he won't win consistently on the intermediate routes that most NFL offenses use to move the chains.

Concentration drops on crossers and in-catch routes are a recurring issue on tape. He takes his eye off the ball early to set up his after-the-catch move, which leads to drops on balls that should be caught. It's a classic YAC-player problem — he's thinking about what happens after the catch before he's actually caught the ball. This is fixable with reps and emphasis, but it costs his quarterback trust and his fantasy floor in the meantime.

The yards-per-catch decline from 14.7 to 14.2 is a minor but measurable trend that could indicate either a marginal loss of deep speed or a defensive adjustment. If defenses are playing him with more over-the-top safety help, his deep-ball efficiency will decline and he'll need to win more underneath. That's fine — his YAC ability translates to shorter routes — but it caps his ceiling as a vertical weapon.
tyson.nflFit:
Tyson is a Z-receiver or slot in a spread system that uses his speed to stress defenses vertically and his YAC ability on schemed touches underneath. He's not an X-receiver who wins on the boundary against press-man — he needs motion, alignment flexibility, and creative play design to get open. The ideal fit is a team with a quarterback who throws a great deep ball and an offensive coordinator who uses receiver motion and formationing to create favorable matchups. He'd thrive in Miami, Detroit, or San Francisco — all teams that use receiver motion and schemed touches as core concepts.

The motion and alignment flexibility are critical because Tyson's best trait — his after-the-catch ability — is maximized when he catches the ball in space. A creative coordinator will use pre-snap motion to get him free releases, align him in the slot against slower nickel backs, and design schemed touches that put the ball in his hands with room to operate. A rigid system that lines him up static on the boundary and asks him to win against press-man will waste his best skills.

The deep speed is the complementary trait that opens up everything underneath. When defenses have to respect Tyson's ability to run by them, they play softer coverage and give him more room to operate on underneath routes. That constraint effect benefits the entire offense, not just Tyson individually. A coordinator who understands this will use Tyson's deep speed as a weapon even on plays where the ball doesn't go to him.
tyson.comp:
The best comparison is DJ Moore — a receiver who wins with YAC ability, alignment flexibility, and deep speed rather than elite route-running or contested-catch ability. Like Moore, Tyson's fantasy value is rooted in getting the ball in space and creating after the catch. He doesn't have Moore's physicality or consistency, but the athletic profile and production arc are similar. The floor comparison is Curtis Samuel — a versatile receiver/returner whose fantasy value depends on creative usage.

The Moore comparison is about the YAC-driven profile. Moore has never been an elite separator or contested-catch winner, but he's produced consistently because he gets the ball in space and creates after the catch. Tyson has a similar skill set — his 14.7 and 14.2 YPR are driven by catch-and-run ability, not just deep balls. If he lands in a system that features him on schemed touches, he could produce Moore-like volume.

The Samuel comparison is the floor. If Tyson lands in a rigid system that doesn't use his motion and alignment flexibility, he becomes a situational deep threat whose fantasy value is limited to boom/bust weeks. Samuel has survived in the NFL because of his versatility; Tyson has the same versatility but needs a coordinator who will exploit it.
tyson.dynastyOutlook:
Tyson is a WR2/3 with weekly WR1 upside if he stays healthy. The knee is the only thing keeping him from the top tier of this class. In PPR, his YAC ability and alignment flexibility give him a solid floor even when the deep ball isn't connecting. In standard, his boom weeks come from the deep touchdowns. His long-term value depends on landing spot — a creative OC who uses him all over the field could make him a top-15 dynasty WR. A rigid system that lines him up static on the boundary could waste his best traits.

The weekly profile will be boom/bust regardless of landing spot. Tyson's production comes from explosive plays — deep touchdowns, long catch-and-run gains, and occasional return contributions. Those plays win weeks but they don't happen every game. Expect 4-5 boom weeks and 8-10 quiet weeks per season, which makes him a better WR2 than WR1 in PPR formats where consistency matters.

The knee risk is the discount factor that makes him interesting. If he were completely healthy with no injury history, he'd be in the top-3 WR conversation with Tate and Lemon. The injury suppresses his draft capital and his ADP, which means you can get top-tier talent at a discount. If the medicals check out, that's a value. If they don't, you've invested in a player whose rookie-year opportunity could be limited.
tyson.badgersFit:
Tyson is a high-ceiling WR who fits exactly what the Badgers need — explosive playmaking from multiple alignments. The knee injury is the risk, but two 1,000-yard seasons post-injury are the strongest possible reassurance. He's a WR2 with WR1 upside at pick 1.08. If you already grabbed Love or Mendoza, Tyson is the perfect WR1 to pair with them because his skill set complements a run-heavy offense — he can win on play-action deep balls and schemed screens that flow naturally from a run-first identity. Don't let the knee scare you away from a receiver who's proven it works.

The specific complement to a run-heavy offense is important. Play-action deep balls are the highest-value passes in football, and Tyson is built to win on those — he tracks the ball over his shoulder and adjusts to underthrows without losing speed. Schemed screens and crossers are the second layer, and those flow naturally from run formations that defenses are already committing to. Tyson's after-the-catch ability turns those schemed touches into explosive gains.

The risk is the knee, but the reward is a top-15 dynasty WR at pick 1.08. If you've already secured Love or Mendoza with your first pick, Tyson is the ideal complement because his skill set is different from what those players provide. You're building a roster with a three-down RB, a dual-threat QB, and a YAC-driven WR — that's a championship core.
tyson.verdict: High ceiling, moderate risk. Worth the pick if you can absorb the injury floor.

lemon.shortReport:
The safest wide receiver pick in this class, and it's not close. Lemon won the Biletnikoff Award in 2025 on the strength of 79 catches, 1,156 yards, and 11 touchdowns — but the award is almost incidental to the scouting profile. What matters is the route-running: Lemon creates separation on every route against every coverage type, not with speed or size, but with technique, timing, and an understanding of leverage that most college receivers don't develop until they've been in an NFL building for two years. He's the kind of receiver who will produce regardless of his quarterback, his scheme, or his situation — and in dynasty, that reliability has enormous compounding value.

The route-running is the foundation of everything Lemon does as a player. Against off-coverage, he eats cushion with stem speed and snaps the route at a depth that forces the corner to open his hips and recover — by the time the corner recovers, Lemon has already caught the ball. Against press-man, he uses hand-fighting and leverage to create a two-way go. Against zone, he finds the soft spots with an instinctive understanding of spacing. The common thread is that he's always open — not by a lot, but by enough. In the NFL, 'enough' separation is all a good quarterback needs.

The fantasy profile is uniquely stable. Lemon's production doesn't depend on deep touchdowns or schemed touches — it depends on route-running that creates consistent targets. That consistency is what wins PPR leagues over the long haul. He'll give you 6-70 every week with the occasional TD bonus, which is a WR2 floor with WR1 upside in the right offense.
lemon.athleticProfile: Lemon is a suddenness-over-speed athlete, which is a profile that translates better to the NFL than many evaluators realize. He doesn't have the long speed to stress defenses vertically — he's not going to run a 4.3 at the combine and he's not going to take the top off an NFL defense. What he has is elite short-area quickness: his hips snap through breaks with a violence that creates instant separation, and he does it without any wasted movement at the stem top. There's no false step, no extra hop, no rounding — he plants, drives, and separates in a single fluid motion. That efficiency is what makes him a quarterback's best friend, because the ball can be thrown on time to a window that Lemon creates consistently. His smaller frame (listed around 5'10" 195) is a limitation against press coverage from bigger corners, but he mitigates it with technique — he uses his hands to defeat the jam, creates leverage with his stem, and wins at the top of the route where his quickness is most relevant. He's not going to out-physical anyone, but he doesn't need to because he's never in a position where physicality is required.
strengths: The route-running is the foundation of his entire profile and it operates on multiple levels simultaneously. Against off-coverage, he uses stem speed to eat cushion and then snaps the route at a depth that forces the corner to open his hips and recover — by the time the corner recovers, Lemon has already caught the ball and turned upfield. Against press-man, he uses hand-fighting and leverage to create a two-way go, then attacks the defender's leverage with a route that takes advantage of the opening he's created. Against zone, he finds the soft spots in the coverage with an instinctive understanding of spacing that you can't teach. The common thread across all three coverage types is that he's always open — not by a lot, but by enough. And in the NFL, 'enough' separation is all a good quarterback needs.

The Biletnikoff Award is a meaningful signal, not just a resume line. Recent winners — Ja'Marr Chase, DeVonta Smith, Jerry Jeudy, Amari Cooper — have translated at an extremely high rate to NFL success. The award tells you that Lemon was the most productive receiver in college football against the best competition, and that his production was consistent enough over a full season to earn the recognition. It's not a guarantee, but it's the strongest possible signal in a wide receiver prospect.

The suddenness out of breaks is a mechanical trait that translates directly to the NFL because it's rooted in body control, not athleticism. Lemon doesn't create separation by being faster than the defender — he creates it by changing direction faster, which is a function of hip flexibility, core strength, and technique. Those are sustainable, coachable, and transferable skills. He'll create the same separation against NFL corners that he created against college corners because the mechanism — snap-and-drive route-running — doesn't depend on the defender's speed.

The hands are reliable in a way that matters for fantasy consistency. He doesn't make acrobatic one-handed catches on highlights — he catches everything in his frame with natural, pluck-and-tuck technique that secures the ball before turning upfield. The drop rate is negligible. In fantasy, this means he converts targets into catches at a high rate, which is the foundation of PPR production. A receiver who catches 75% of his targets is more valuable than a receiver who catches 55% of his targets even if the latter has more highlight-reel plays.

The production trajectory — 23 catches to 52 to 79, zero touchdowns to 6 to 11 — is the kind of year-over-year improvement that signals genuine development rather than a single lucky season. Every metric improved every year: catches, yards, touchdowns, yards per route run. That's not a fluke; that's a player getting better.
weaknesses: The lack of deep speed is a real ceiling cap that no amount of route-running can fully overcome. In the NFL, the most valuable receivers are the ones who can win on all three levels — short, intermediate, and deep. Lemon wins decisively on the short and intermediate levels but offers nothing on the deep ball. He can't take the top off a defense, which means safeties can cheat toward the line of scrimmage without fear of being burned over the top. That constraint affects the entire offense, not just Lemon's individual production.

The smaller frame creates matchup problems against physical press-man corners who can disrupt his release at the line of scrimmage. Lemon uses technique to mitigate this — hand-fighting, leverage creation, varied splits — but there are corners in the NFL (think Marshon Lattimore, Jaire Alexander) who are both physical and quick enough to erase his technique advantage. On those Sundays, Lemon may be a non-factor, which creates boom/bust tendencies in his weekly fantasy production that his overall consistency masks.

The USC system inflated his numbers to some degree. Lincoln Riley's offense creates schemed touches — screens, RPO pop passes, motion-crossers — that generate easy completions and yards after the catch. Lemon benefited from these concepts, and some of his production is a product of the system rather than purely individual talent. NFL defenses will take away the easy schemed touches and force him to win on traditional route concepts. He can do that — his route-running is legitimate — but the volume may decline when the schemed touches disappear.

The yards-per-reception ceiling (13.7, 14.7, 14.6 across three seasons) is capped by his lack of deep-ball ability. He's a possession-and-move-the-chains receiver whose fantasy value is rooted in volume and efficiency rather than explosive plays. That's a fine profile — it's the profile of a WR2/3 who gives you 70-80 catches and 900-1,100 yards every year — but it's not a WR1 profile. In dynasty, you're drafting a reliable starter, not a league-winner.
lemon.nflFit: Lemon is a scheme-proof receiver whose route-running translates to any system. He can play the slot in a spread offense, the Z in a timing-based West Coast system, or even the flanker in a pro-style offense. His ideal role is as a quarterback's security blanket — the receiver who moves the chains on third down, wins in the red zone with precise route-running, and produces consistently regardless of game script. He doesn't need a creative coordinator or a great quarterback to produce, which makes him one of the safest picks in this class. The ideal fit is a team with a precise, timing-based quarterback — think Tua Tagovailoa, Kirk Cousins, or Brock Purdy — who throws on time and trusts his receivers to be where they're supposed to be.
lemon.comp: The ceiling comparison is CeeDee Lamb — a receiver who wins with route nuance, body control, and reliability rather than elite athleticism. Lemon doesn't have Lamb's after-the-catch creativity or his versatility as a punt returner, but the route-running profile is similar. The floor comparison is Keenan Allen — a precise route-runner who produces consistently but lacks the explosive element that elevates him to the top tier of the position.
lemon.dynastyOutlook: Lemon is a top-3 dynasty WR in this class with a safe floor and a capped ceiling. He'll give you 70-80 catches and 900-1,100 yards every year, which is WR2/3 production in PPR formats. He'll rarely have a zero-catch week, which makes him an ideal flex play in best-ball formats. The Biletnikoff Award is a strong signal that his production is legitimate. The ceiling is capped without deep speed, but the floor is as high as any receiver in this class. In PPR, he's a top-15 dynasty WR. In standard, the lack of big plays drops him into the WR2/3 range.
lemon.badgersFit: Lemon is the safest WR pick you can make in this draft, and at pick 1.20 he's a steal — he should go higher. The route-running means he'll produce regardless of his quarterback or scheme, which is invaluable in dynasty where you can't control the landing spot. He's your WR1 if you went RB/QB early, or an elite WR2 if you took Tate or Tyson first. The floor is 70 catches and 900 yards; the ceiling is a Biletnikoff-caliber season. You're not drafting Lemon for the ceiling — you're drafting him because he'll never tank your week. In PPR, that reliability compounds over a 17-game season into a top-15 WR finish. Don't overthink it.
lemon.verdict: The safest WR pick in this class. Route-running translates, floor is elite.

price.shortReport: The most fascinating medical-risk prospect in this class. Price tore his Achilles in October 2023 and returned by August 2024 — a timeline that borders on miraculous for a running back whose game is built on explosive burst. The 2025 tape confirms the burst is back: he hits the hole with the same urgency and suddenness he showed before the injury, and his 11 touchdowns on just 113 carries prove he hasn't lost his nose for the end zone. But the Achilles cloud hangs over everything — the re-injury risk, the long-term durability questions, and the fact that he's never proven he can handle a full workload even when healthy. He's a high-upside RB2 if the medicals check out, but the floor is a player who can't stay on the field.
price.athleticProfile: Price's defining trait is his first-step burst — the kind of suddenness that makes linebackers take a false step and safeties arrive a half-second late. Before the Achilles tear, he was one of the most explosive runners in college football, reaching top speed in two strides and maintaining it through contact. The post-injury tape shows that burst has returned, though there are subtle differences: his cut ability is slightly less violent on break-back runs, and he seems to favor his right side on zone reads where he used to cut either direction with equal facility. These are minor observations that most evaluators won't notice, but they're the kind of things that matter at the NFL level where the margin for error is smaller. His smaller frame (listed around 5'10" 195) is a durability concern independent of the Achilles — he's not built to absorb 250 carries and 50 targets over a 17-game season. The receiving upside is real: his hands are natural, he catches the ball away from his body, and he's shown the ability to adjust to poorly thrown balls out of the backfield. The 22-187-2TD receiving line in 2025 is modest in volume but encouraging in efficiency — he's converting those catches into first downs and touchdowns at a high rate.
strengths: The burst is the foundation of his entire profile and it's back. Watch the 2025 Notre Dame tape and you'll see Price hit the hole with the same urgency he showed before the Achilles tear. He doesn't hesitate — he commits to the gap and explodes through it. That decisiveness is critical because it means he's not playing scared or compensating for the injury. He's trusting his body again, and the results (6.0 YPC, 11 TDs) confirm that trust is warranted.

The goal-line finishing is instinctive and refined. Price doesn't try to run over defenders at the goal line — he reads the leverage of the defensive line, identifies the soft shoulder, and squeezes through gaps that don't look open from the broadcast angle. Eleven touchdowns on 113 carries is an absurd efficiency rate that speaks to his spatial awareness and finishing ability near the end zone. That skill translates to any level because it's rooted in vision and patience, not just power.

The receiving upside is emerging as a legitimate third-down skill. In 2025, he caught 22 passes for 187 yards and two touchdowns, showing natural hands and the ability to adjust to balls thrown behind or above him. His route-running is still basic — mostly angle routes, swing screens, and check-downs — but the hands and body control suggest he can develop into a legitimate pass-catching option. If he can add option routes and wheel routes to his repertoire, he has the profile of a three-down back in the right system.

The Achilles recovery timeline is remarkable and suggests elite work ethic and physical resilience. Returning from an October 2023 tear to play in August 2024 is a 10-month turnaround for a position that typically requires 12-14 months. That he not only returned but produced effectively (7 TDs in 2024) tells you something about his dedication to rehabilitation and his body's ability to heal. That matters for dynasty because it suggests he'll attack NFL rehab with the same intensity if he suffers another injury.
weaknesses: The Achilles injury is the elephant in the room and it can't be dismissed with post-injury production alone. Medical science tells us that Achilles tears carry an elevated re-injury risk for the first 2-3 years after surgery, and that running backs who rely on explosive burst are particularly vulnerable because the tendon bears the highest load during acceleration. Price's game is built on burst. If the tendon fails again, his career could be over. That's not a minor risk — it's an existential one for his fantasy value.

He's never handled a full workload. His career high in carries is 113 in 2025. He's never had 120 carries in a season, never had 200 rushing yards in a game, never been the bellcow for a full 13-game college season. The NFL asks running backs to carry the ball 250+ times and catch 50+ passes over 17 games. We have zero evidence that Price's body can tolerate that volume, and the Achilles injury only amplifies the concern.

Pass protection is a technical deficit that will limit his third-down snaps in the NFL. He leans into rushers with his shoulder instead of sitting into his stance and mirroring with his feet, which gets him washed by inside stunts and late blitzes. Until he improves this, NFL coordinators will substitute him out on third-and-long, which caps his receiving volume and therefore his PPR floor.

The YPC decline from 6.9 in 2024 to 6.0 in 2025 could be noise, but it could also be a sign that defenses adjusted to his burst and forced him to run into heavier boxes. If the latter, it suggests his efficiency is dependent on favorable defensive fronts — something he won't always get in the NFL.
price.nflFit:
Price is a change-of-pace or committee back with RB2 upside if he lands in the right situation. He needs a zone-run scheme that maximizes his burst on outside-zone and stretch concepts, and a passing game that features him as a check-down and screen option. He's not a three-down back in the traditional sense — he's a rotational weapon who can produce explosive plays in limited snaps. The ideal fit is a team that already has a between-the-tackles grinder and needs a speed complement — think Buffalo, Kansas City, or San Francisco.

The zone-scheme dependency is real. Price's burst is maximized when he's running on the move toward the boundary — outside-zone, stretch, and toss concepts that allow him to use his acceleration without having to press a predetermined gap. In a gap-scheme power running game, his burst is wasted because the hole is predetermined and he's supposed to hit it north-south regardless of what the defense does. He needs the freedom to read and react, which is what zone concepts provide.

The receiving role is the key to his PPR value. Price caught 22 balls in 2025, and his hands are natural enough to project growth in that area. If he lands with a coordinator who features him on angle routes, swing screens, and check-downs, he could catch 35-40 passes as a rotational back, which gives him a PPR floor that most committee backs lack.
price.comp:
The best comparison is James Cook — a burst-first running back with receiving upside who operates best as part of a committee. Like Cook, Price's fantasy value is maximized in PPR formats where his receiving production adds a floor. He doesn't have Cook's elusiveness in space or his route-running polish, but the athletic profile and role projection are similar. The floor comparison is Cam Akers — a talented back whose career has been derailed by injury.

The Cook comparison is about the role projection specifically. Cook has thrived as part of a committee in Buffalo because his burst and receiving ability give him a defined role even when he's not the lead back. Price projects to a similar role — a rotational weapon who produces explosive plays in limited snaps and contributes in the passing game. The difference is that Cook has stayed healthy; Price hasn't.

The Akers comparison is the injury floor. Akers had first-round talent but his career has been defined by the Achilles tear he suffered in 2021. Price is in a similar position — the talent is real, but the medical history could define his career trajectory. If the Achilles holds up, Price could outperform his draft position significantly. If it doesn't, he could follow the Akers arc.
price.dynastyOutlook:
Price is an RB3/4 with weekly RB2 upside if he earns a role and stays healthy. The injury floor is real — he could be out of the league in two years if the Achilles fails again. But the upside is equally real — 11 touchdowns on 113 carries is elite efficiency that translates to a goal-line role at minimum. In PPR, the 22 catches add a floor that makes him startable in flex spots. His value is entirely dependent on the medical evaluation and the landing spot.

The range of outcomes is wider than almost any player in this class. Best case: he lands in a zone-scheme committee, stays healthy, and produces 800-900 total yards with 8-10 TDs and 30+ catches — an RB2 in PPR. Worst case: the Achilles fails again and he's off fantasy rosters entirely. That variance makes him a polarizing pick — some managers will love the upside at his ADP, others won't touch him.

The PPR premium matters. In standard scoring, Price is a TD-dependent dart throw with limited volume. In PPR, the 22 catches give him a weekly floor that makes him rosterable even when he's not scoring. That floor is the difference between a usable flex play and an unstartable bench stash.
price.badgersFit:
Price is the kind of high-risk, high-reward pick that can win you a dynasty championship or leave you with an empty roster spot. The 11 touchdowns on 113 carries is elite efficiency, but he's never proven he can handle a full workload and the Achilles injury is a permanent concern. At pick 2.32 he's worth the dart — especially if you already have Love, because Price becomes a premium handcuff with standalone flex value. The receiving upside (22 catches in 2025) gives him PPR appeal that most handcuffs lack. But don't draft him as your RB2 — draft him as your RB3/4 and hope the upside materializes.

The Love handcuff angle is the key Badgers-specific value. If you've already invested a first-round pick in Love, spending a late second on his backup is smart roster construction. If Love goes down, Price steps into the same zone-scheme role and produces immediately. If Love stays healthy, Price still has standalone flex value in PPR because of the receiving upside.

The risk is real but the price is right. At pick 2.32, you're not investing a premium asset — you're spending a dart on a player whose upside is an RB2. If he busts, you've lost a late second. If he hits, you've got a league-winner at a discount. That's the kind of risk-reward profile that wins dynasty leagues.
price.verdict: High-upside RB2 if the medicals check out. The Achilles is the swing factor.

concepcion.shortReport: A gadget slot receiver whose fantasy value is rooted in what he can do with the ball in his hands rather than how he gets it. Concepcion's 2023 season at NC State — 71 catches, 839 yards, 10 touchdowns — was one of the most productive slot seasons in college football that year, and it was built entirely on after-the-catch creativity and schemed touches. The transfer to Texas A&M revealed a more modest version of the same player: 55-661-8 in 2025 is solid but not the same explosive weapon. The punt-return touchdown in 2023 adds a dimension of value that most slot receivers lack, but his small frame and boundary limitations cap his ceiling at WR4/5 in most dynasty formats.
concepcion.athleticProfile: Concepcion is a twitchy, sudden athlete whose movement profile is optimized for the slot. His short-area quickness is elite — he can stop and restart in a single stride, which is the foundation of his after-the-catch ability and his route-running on option routes and angle routes. He doesn't have the long speed to stress defenses vertically; his game is entirely contained within 15 yards of the line of scrimmage. His frame is small — listed around 5'10" 185 — which limits his effectiveness against press coverage at the line and over the middle against NFL linebackers who run 4.5. He's not going to out-physical anyone, and he's not going to run away from anyone in a straight line. What he does is create in space — the first defender in the open field rarely makes the tackle because Concepcion shifts his hips and accelerates past the tackle point before the defender can adjust.
strengths: The after-the-catch ability is his most translatable skill because it doesn't depend on scheme or quarterback quality. Give Concepcion the ball on a screen, a slant, or a crosser, and he'll make the first defender miss with a hip-shift that creates a half-second of separation. That half-second is enough for him to accelerate past the second level and turn a 5-yard catch into a 20-yard gain. The mechanism is consistent — it's not a variety of moves, it's one move executed at a high level — and that consistency means it will translate to the NFL even against faster defenders.

The route-running in the slot is sudden and precise. He runs option routes, angle routes, and quick-out concepts with the kind of sharp breaks that create instant separation against man coverage. He doesn't round off his routes — he plants, drives, and separates. That precision makes him a quarterback-friendly target in the slot because the ball can be thrown on time to a window that Concepcion creates consistently.

The versatility adds hidden fantasy value that doesn't show up in the receiving line. He returns punts (one touchdown in 2023), takes jet sweeps and end-arounds, and can be used as a decoy in motion to create favorable formations for other receivers. In fantasy, that versatility means he has a roster floor even if the offense doesn't feature him as a receiver — he'll contribute on special teams and gadget plays that add a point or two per week in return-yard leagues.

The production at two programs — 71-839-10 at NC State and 55-661-8 at Texas A&M — proves his skill set translates across systems and competition levels. He produced in the ACC and the SEC, which are the two best conferences for defensive talent. The volume decline at A&M is concerning but the efficiency was still solid.
weaknesses: The small frame is a hard ceiling on his NFL role. He can't win on the boundary against press-man corners who outweigh him by 30 pounds and out-reach him by four inches. He can't go over the middle against NFL linebackers who run 4.5 and hit like trucks. He's a slot-only player, which limits his snap count to roughly 60% of offensive plays in most systems. That snap count cap translates directly to a volume cap in fantasy.

The production decline from NC State to Texas A&M is a legitimate concern when you dig into the context. At NC State, he was the focal point of the passing game — the offense was designed to get him the ball in space. At Texas A&M, he was one of several weapons in a more balanced attack, and his production dipped accordingly. That suggests his 2023 season was more a product of opportunity and scheme than pure individual talent. In the NFL, he's unlikely to be the focal point of any offense, which means the NC State version of Concepcion is probably his ceiling, not his floor.

He's not a deep threat and he never will be. His entire game operates within 15 yards of the line of scrimmage, which means defenses can compress their coverage and take away the underneath throws without worrying about him beating them over the top. That constraint limits his weekly ceiling — he'll give you 5-6 catches for 60 yards most weeks, which is a WR4/5 line in PPR and irrelevant in standard.

The gadget/slot-only profile means his fantasy value is highly dependent on how creative his offensive coordinator is. A coordinator who uses motion, schemed touches, and formationing to get him the ball in space will maximize his value. A coordinator who lines him up static in the slot and runs him on basic routes will waste his best traits.
concepcion.nflFit:
Concepcion is a slot receiver and returner whose value is maximized in a spread offense that uses schemed touches and receiver motion. He needs a quarterback who gets the ball out quickly — a timing-based passer who throws on schedule to the slot window. The return ability gives him a roster floor even if the offense doesn't feature him as a receiver. The ideal fit is a team with a quick-release quarterback and a creative slot-heavy passing game — think Kansas City, Seattle, or New England.

The coordinator dependency is the most important factor in his fantasy projection. A creative coordinator who uses motion, schemed touches, and formationing to get Concepcion the ball in space will maximize his after-the-catch ability. A rigid coordinator who lines him up static in the slot and runs him on basic routes will waste his best trait. The difference between a WR3 season and a WR5 season for Concepcion is almost entirely dependent on how his OC uses him.

The return ability gives him a roster floor that insulates against the worst-case scenario. Even if the offense doesn't feature him as a receiver, he'll contribute on special teams — and in return-yard leagues, that contribution has direct fantasy value. That floor is what makes him draftable in the late rounds of dynasty rookie drafts.
concepcion.comp:
The best comparison is Tank Dell — a small, sudden slot receiver who creates after the catch and contributes on returns. Like Dell, Concepcion's fantasy value is rooted in YAC ability and schemed usage rather than traditional receiving skills. He doesn't have Dell's deep speed or his catch radius, but the slot/return profile is similar. The floor comparison is Braxton Berrios — a slot/returner who produces in fits and starts.

The Dell comparison is about the slot/return profile and the YAC-driven production. Dell has been productive in Houston because the offense features him on schemed touches that put the ball in his hands with room to operate. Concepcion needs the same kind of creative usage to produce. The difference is that Dell has more deep speed and a larger catch radius, which gives him a higher ceiling.

The Berrios comparison is the floor. Berrios has survived in the NFL because he contributes on special teams and occasionally produces as a slot receiver. Concepcion has a similar profile — he'll stick on a roster because of his return ability, but his fantasy value will fluctuate with his offensive role.
concepcion.dynastyOutlook:
Concepcion is a WR4/5 with weekly WR3 upside in the right matchup. The return value adds a floor in return-yard leagues. In standard PPR leagues, he's a fringe roster player who'll give you 5-6 catches for 60 yards on his good weeks and disappear on his bad ones. His value is higher in best-ball formats where his boom weeks count without his bust weeks hurting you.

The weekly variance is high because his production depends on how the coordinator uses him. In weeks where the game script favors schemed touches — screens, crossers, motion-based concepts — Concepcion can produce a WR3 line. In weeks where the offense goes vertical and doesn't feature the slot, he'll give you 2-20-0. That variance makes him unstartable in most weekly-lineup formats but viable in best-ball.

The return value is the insurance policy. Even in weeks where he doesn't produce as a receiver, Concepcion will contribute on special teams. In return-yard leagues, that contribution adds 2-3 points per week that most slot receivers don't provide. That floor makes him more rosterable than his receiving production alone would suggest.
concepcion.badgersFit:
Concepcion is a useful dynasty piece but not a building block. He's a slot-only gadget player who gives you WR3 weeks when the matchup is right and the coordinator features him. At pick 2.24 he's decent value if you already have your WR1/2 locked in. The punt-return ability is a bonus in return-yard leagues. Draft him for depth, not as a starter — and don't expect the NC State version (71-839-10) to show up in the NFL. That was scheme and opportunity, not pure talent.

The Badgers-specific value is the complement to your bigger receivers. If you've already drafted Tate and Tyson, Concepcion gives you a completely different skill set — a slot gadget player who wins with YAC and schemed touches rather than size and speed. That diversity of skill sets makes your roster more resilient to different defensive matchups.

The risk is that he's a coordinator-dependent player whose production fluctuates with usage. In a good offense with a creative coordinator, he's a WR4 with WR3 upside.
