Douglas's 52 catches in that system may translate to 35-40 in a more balanced NFL offense, which caps his PPR floor. But the deep-ball tracking skill is scheme-independent — he'll catch deep balls regardless of how many times the offense throws. The question is whether he gets enough targets to produce consistently.
strengths: The deep-ball tracking is his most translatable skill. He adjusts to the ball in flight with natural hands and body control, making him a reliable target on go-balls and post routes even when the throw isn't perfect. That tracking ability translates to the NFL because it's rooted in hand-eye coordination and spatial awareness, not just speed.

The ascending production (11→40→52 catches) shows he's gradually earning a larger role. The improvement from 11 catches as a freshman to 52 as a junior suggests he's developing as a route runner, not just running faster than defenders.
weaknesses: He's essentially a one-dimensional deep-ball specialist. His route tree is limited to go-balls, posts, and occasional seam routes — he doesn't run the curl-stop, comeback, or option routes that would expand his role and increase his volume. Until he develops a more complete route tree, he's a situational deep threat whose snap count will be limited.

The volume ceiling is modest. Fifty-two catches in a season gives him a terrible PPR floor — he'll have weeks where he catches 1 ball for 30 yards and weeks where he catches 5 for 120 and a touchdown. That boom/bust profile makes him unstartable in most weeks.
douglas.nflFit:
Deep-ball Z-receiver in a vertical offense that takes shots downfield. He's a situational weapon whose value is concentrated in specific game scripts — when the offense is trailing and needs big plays, or when the defense is playing single-high safety.

The situational role caps his snap count. Douglas will play roughly 40-50% of offensive snaps — vertical shot plays, obvious passing downs, and trailing game scripts. That snap count limits his target volume to roughly 50-60 per season, which caps his PPR floor regardless of his efficiency.

The deep-ball tracking is the one skill that translates regardless of scheme. He adjusts to the ball in flight with natural hands and body control, which means he'll catch deep balls even when the throw isn't perfect. That skill is valuable in any vertical offense.
douglas.comp:
Mack Hollins — a deep-ball specialist with limited volume who produces in boom/bust fashion. Like Hollins, Douglas will win you 2-3 weeks a year and be invisible the rest.

The Hollins comparison is about the boom/bust deep-ball profile. Hollins has survived in the NFL because he can win on go-balls and post routes, but his volume is too low for consistent fantasy production. Douglas has a similar profile — he'll give you 4-120-1 weeks and 1-20-0 weeks with no way to predict which version you'll get.
douglas.dynastyOutlook:
WR5/6 with boom weeks. The YPR is enticing but the volume caps his floor. He's a bench stash for the weeks when the deep ball connects.

The weekly variance is extreme. Douglas will give you 2-3 boom weeks per season where he catches 4-5 balls for 100+ yards and a TD, and 14 quiet weeks where he catches 1-2 balls for 30 yards. That profile makes him a better best-ball pick than a weekly-lineup asset.

The ascending production (11→40→52 catches) is encouraging but the volume ceiling is still modest. Even if he improves to 60 catches, that's a WR4 in PPR with boom/bust variance. The ceiling is limited by his route tree.
douglas.badgersFit:
Douglas is a boom/bust WR5 with big-play ability. The 16+ YPR means when he catches it, it's a big gain, but 52 catches gives him a low PPR floor. At pick 4.75 he's a fine dart for your bench — don't start him unless you need a boom week. He'll either hit 2-3 big games or be off your roster by October.

The Badgers-specific value is the complement to your possession receivers. If you've drafted Lemon and Cooper for PPR floor, Douglas gives you a completely different profile — a boom/bust deep-ball specialist who can win weeks when your floor receivers are quiet. That diversity makes your roster more resilient.

The risk is the volume. Fifty-two catches in college doesn't project to consistent fantasy production in the NFL. He needs to expand his route tree to earn more targets, and there's no guarantee that development will happen.
douglas.verdict: Deep-ball specialist. Boom/bust WR5 for bench stash.

bernard.shortReport:
A versatile receiver whose ascending production across two programs (41-419-1TD at Washington in 2023, 50-685-5TD in 2024, then 60-813-7TD at Alabama in 2025) tells a story of steady development that deserves more credit than it gets. Bernard doesn't have a single elite trait — no game-breaking speed, no dominant size, no sudden separation ability — but he's improved every measurable metric every year, and the jump from Washington to Alabama didn't slow him down. Sixty catches and 7 TDs at Alabama is real production against elite SEC defenses.

The Alabama context cuts both ways. On one hand, the Crimson Tide's offense creates schemed openings — motion, bunch formations, RPOs — that inflate receiver production. Bernard benefited from those schematic advantages, and some of his numbers are a product of the system rather than individual talent. On the other hand, earning 60 targets at Alabama means you beat out other talented receivers for those targets. The coaching staff chose to throw him the ball 60 times, which is a vote of confidence in his ability. That selection pressure matters because it means NFL scouts can trust that the production was earned, not inherited.

The kick-return contribution (8-167 in 2024) adds a hidden value dimension that most receivers in this tier can't match. Return specialists stick on NFL rosters because they contribute on special teams, which gives Bernard a roster floor even if his receiving production is modest. In dynasty, that floor matters because it means he won't be cut — he'll have a role even if it's not a fantasy-relevant one. The lack of a defining trait caps his ceiling, but the steady improvement and roster-floor insurance make him a useful depth piece.
strengths: The ascending production shows he's earning trust within the offense. Going from 41 to 60 catches across three seasons suggests coaches are finding more ways to use him — jet sweeps, return duties, and schemed touches that add hidden value. The kick-return contribution (8-167 in 2024) adds a dimension that gives him a roster floor even if the receiving production is modest.
The improved TD production (1→5→7) suggests he's finding ways to contribute in the red zone, even if it's through schemed touches rather than individual playmaking.
weaknesses: He lacks any standout trait — no elite speed, size, or suddenness that would create separation against NFL defensive backs. He's a jack-of-all-trades whose versatility is his calling card but whose lack of a defining skill caps his ceiling at every level.

The Alabama system inflated his production. The Crimson Tide's offense creates schemed openings — motion, bunch formations, RPOs — that generate clean releases and open windows for receivers. Bernard benefited from these schematic advantages, and his production may decline when he's not surrounded by elite talent.
bernard.nflFit:
Depth WR with return value. Best as a WR4/5 who contributes on special teams and provides versatile depth. He's a valuable NFL role player but a limited fantasy asset.

The return value is the roster-floor insurance. Bernard will stick on an NFL roster because he contributes on special teams — kick returns, coverage units, and gadget plays. That floor is important because it means he'll have a role even if the receiving production is modest.

The lack of a defining trait caps his ceiling. Bernard doesn't have elite speed, size, or suddenness, which means he can't create his own separation against NFL defensive backs. His production depends on schemed openings and favorable matchups, which limits his fantasy upside.
bernard.comp:
Kendrick Bourne — a reliable depth WR who produces modestly without elite traits. Like Bourne, Bernard's value is in his versatility and reliability rather than his playmaking ability.

The Bourne comparison is about the depth-receiver profile. Bourne has produced in the NFL because he's reliable and versatile — he can play multiple positions and contribute on special teams. Bernard has a similar profile, but he lacks Bourne's route-running polish, which limits his receiving upside even further.
bernard.dynastyOutlook:
WR6/7. Fringe roster player with return-league value. He'll stick on an NFL roster because of his versatility, but he won't produce enough to be a consistent fantasy starter.

The return-league premium is the only thing that makes him rosterable. In standard scoring, Bernard is a fringe player who'll give you 40-50 catches for 500 yards. In return-yard leagues, he adds kick-return contributions that elevate him to a usable WR5/6.

The Alabama system inflation is a concern. The Crimson Tide's offense creates schemed openings that inflate receiver production. Bernard's numbers may decline when he's not surrounded by elite talent and creative scheming.
bernard.badgersFit:
Bernard is a depth piece with return value. The ascending catches are nice but the lack of elite traits caps his ceiling. At pick 4.47 he's a reach — better WRs are available in this range. Only draft in return-yard leagues.

The Badgers-specific concern is the value relative to the pick. At pick 4.47, you can get players with higher ceilings and more defined roles. Bernard's versatility is nice but it doesn't translate to fantasy production — he's a jack-of-all-trades who's a master of none.

The return value is the only saving grace. In return-yard leagues, Bernard's kick-return contributions add 2-3 points per week that most WR6/7 types don't provide. That floor makes him more rosterable than his receiving production alone would suggest.
bernard.verdict: Versatile depth WR without elite traits. Return-league value only.

boston.shortReport:
A red-zone monster whose touchdown production (21 TDs in two seasons after barely playing as a sophomore) is the fantasy hook that demands attention. Boston waited behind Odunze, McMillan, and Polk at Washington, then exploded with 63-834-9TD in 2024 and 60-821-12TD in 2025 — proving that the talent was always there, it just needed opportunity. The 12 touchdowns on 60 catches in 2025 (a 20% TD rate) is unsustainable, but the skill that produced it is very real.

What makes Boston's TD production believable is the specificity of how he scores. He wins in the red zone with three distinct skills that translate to any level: strong hands that clamp the ball and refuse to let defenders rip it out, perfect leaping timing that allows him to catch the ball at its highest point, and an innate sense for finding the soft spot in zone coverage near the goal line. Those aren't fluky skills that disappear against better competition — they're technique-driven abilities that will produce touchdowns at the NFL level, just at a lower rate. Expect 7-9 TDs per season instead of 12, which still makes him a useful fantasy asset.

The volume is the underappreciated dimension of his profile. Boston caught 60+ balls in back-to-back seasons, which gives him a PPR floor that pure TD-dependent receivers lack. He's not just a goal-line specialist who disappears between the 20s — he's a productive receiver who happens to be exceptional near the end zone. That combination of volume and TD efficiency is rare in this tier, and it's why Boston is a better dynasty asset than his 'TD-dependent' label suggests.
strengths: The red-zone dominance is rooted in three specific skills that translate to the NFL. First, he has strong hands that clamp the ball and refuse to let defenders rip it out. Second, he times his jump perfectly to catch the ball at its highest point, which makes him a reliable target on fade routes and back-shoulder throws. Third, he has an innate sense for finding the soft spot in zone coverage near the goal line — he drifts to the open window and presents a clean target for his quarterback. Those three skills together produced 27 touchdowns in three seasons, and they'll produce touchdowns at the next level too.

The volume is solid — 60+ catches in back-to-back seasons — which gives him a PPR floor that pure TD-dependent receivers lack. He's not just a goal-line specialist; he's a productive receiver who happens to be exceptional near the end zone.
weaknesses: The TD rate is unsustainable and regression is inevitable. Even the best red-zone receivers in the NFL score on roughly 12-15% of their catches. Boston's 20% rate in 2025 will come down to 12-14%, which means 7-8 touchdowns instead of 12. That's still productive but changes his fantasy profile from 'elite TD scorer' to 'good TD scorer with moderate volume.'

The modest YPR (13.2-13.9) shows he's not a deep-ball threat. His production is concentrated in the short and intermediate areas, which means he's dependent on volume and touchdowns rather than explosive plays. In weeks where the volume is low and the TDs don't come, he'll give you 4-50, which is a WR6 line.
boston.nflFit:
Red-zone X-receiver in an offense that needs a jump-ball target. TD dependency makes him boom/bust in fantasy, but the red-zone skill ensures he'll have a role in any offense that throws in the scoring area.

The red-zone role is his NFL floor. Even if his separation is limited against NFL corners, his ability to find the soft spot in zone coverage and finish in traffic will earn him snaps inside the 20. That role guarantees him some target volume regardless of his overall snap count.

The TD dependency is the fantasy constraint. Boston will produce when he scores and disappear when he doesn't. That profile makes him a better best-ball pick than a weekly-lineup asset, because his boom weeks count without his bust weeks hurting you.
boston.comp:
Tim Patrick — a TD-dependent X-receiver with reliable hands who produces in the red zone but whose fantasy value fluctuates with his touchdown production.

The Patrick comparison is about the red-zone profile specifically. Patrick was a productive fantasy receiver in Denver because he caught TDs consistently — he found the soft spot in zone coverage and finished in traffic. Boston has a similar skill set, though he's smaller than Patrick was, which could limit his effectiveness against NFL-caliber defenders.
boston.dynastyOutlook:
WR4/5 whose value is concentrated in TD-heavy scoring formats. The TD rate will regress from 20% to 12-14%, but the skill is real and the volume (60+ catches) gives him a PPR floor that pure TD-dependent receivers lack.

The weekly profile will be boom/bust. In weeks where Boston scores, he's a WR2. In weeks where he doesn't, he gives you 4-50, which is a WR5 line. That variance makes him a better best-ball pick than a weekly-lineup asset.

The volume is the underrated element. Sixty catches per season gives him a PPR baseline that most TD-dependent receivers can't match. Even with TD regression to 7-8 per season, 60 catches and 800 yards is a usable WR3 in PPR.
boston.badgersFit:
Boston is the best TD-upside WR in this tier. 21 TDs in two seasons at Washington is elite scoring production, and the 60+ catch volume gives him a PPR floor most TD-dependent WRs lack. At pick 4.39 he's worth the investment — the red-zone skill is real and translatable. Draft him as your WR3/4 and enjoy the TD weeks.

The Badgers-specific value is the combination of volume and TDs at a reasonable price. At pick 4.39, you're getting a player who catches 60 balls and scores 7-9 TDs per season. That's a usable flex play every week with boom weeks sprinkled in.

The TD regression is coming — 12 TDs won't repeat — but even at 7-8 TDs with 60 catches, he's a solid WR4 in PPR.
boston.verdict: TD-dependent WR with solid volume. WR4 with boom weeks.

brazzell.shortReport:
A deep-ball specialist whose Tulane-to-Tennessee transfer added SEC credibility to his profile but didn't dramatically change what he is: a vertical threat who wins down the field but can't produce consistently at the intermediate level. Brazzell's 17.8 and 17.6 YPR at Tulane in 2023-24 dropped to 14.0 at Tennessee in 2025, which suggests his route tree expanded but his per-catch value declined as he moved to a more balanced receiving role. The 46-645-6TD line in 2025 is solid SEC production that proves he can compete against elite corners.

The Tennessee transition is the most instructive element of his evaluation. At Tulane, Brazzell was a pure deep-ball specialist — he ran go routes and posts, caught the ball downfield, and produced 17+ YPR on limited volume (40 and 24 catches). At Tennessee, the coaching staff asked him to run a fuller route tree including intermediate concepts — curls, digs, crossers — and the results were mixed. His catches increased from 24 to 46, but his YPR dropped from 17.6 to 14.0, which means the added routes diluted his efficiency without producing enough volume to compensate. That's a concerning development pattern for the NFL, where he'll be asked to run an even more complex route tree.

The 6 TDs across 46 catches in 2025 show he can still finish when he gets to the red zone, but the path there is less explosive than it was at Tulane. The dynasty question is whether the expanded route tree makes him a more complete receiver with a higher floor, or whether it just turned a lethal deep-ball specialist into a mediocre intermediate receiver. The truth is probably both — he's more well-rounded but less explosive, which makes him a harder player to project.
strengths: The deep-ball ability is legitimate and it's rooted in ball-tracking rather than pure speed. Brazzell doesn't run by corners — he adjusts to the ball in flight with the kind of late hands and body control that make him a reliable target on underthrown deep balls. That's a more translatable skill than pure straight-line speed because it works even when the quarterback doesn't throw a perfect ball.

The expanding route tree in 2025 is an underappreciated development. At Tulane, he was purely a go-ball and post route runner — a one-dimensional deep threat. At Tennessee, he added curl-stop, comeback, and dig routes to his repertoire, which is why his YPR dropped (he was running shorter routes) but his overall game improved. That development trajectory suggests he could become a more complete receiver at the NFL level.
weaknesses: The volume inconsistency at Tennessee is a real concern. Going from 40 catches to 24 to 46 suggests his role in the offense fluctuated based on game plan, opponent, and quarterback preference. In the NFL, inconsistent volume means inconsistent fantasy production — he'll have weeks where he's featured and weeks where he's invisible.

The efficiency decline (17.8→17.6→14.0 YPR) is the cost of expanding his route tree, but it also reveals that his best plays come on vertical concepts. As he runs more intermediate routes, his per-catch average will continue to decline, which caps his standard-scoring value even as his PPR floor improves slightly.
brazzell.nflFit:
Deep-ball Z-receiver in a vertical offense. He's a situational weapon whose value is concentrated in specific game scripts.

The situational role caps his snap count. Brazzell will play roughly 40-50% of offensive snaps — vertical shot plays and obvious passing downs. That snap count limits his target volume to roughly 50-60 per season, which caps his PPR floor.

The deep-ball tracking remains his most translatable skill. He adjusts to the ball in flight with natural hands and body control, which means he'll catch deep balls even when the throw isn't perfect. That skill is valuable in any vertical offense regardless of his role expansion.
brazzell.comp:
Darius Slayton — deep-ball speed with limited but growing volume. Like Slayton, Brazzell will give you boom weeks when the deep ball connects and quiet weeks when it doesn't.

The Slayton comparison is about the YPR-driven profile. Slayton has survived in the NFL because he can win downfield, even though his volume is inconsistent. Brazzell has a similar skill set with the added dimension of an expanding route tree, which gives him slightly more upside than a pure deep-ball specialist.
brazzell.dynastyOutlook:
WR5/6 with boom weeks. The YPR is enticing but the volume is inconsistent. He's a bench stash for the weeks when the vertical passing game connects.

The volume inconsistency is the key concern. Going from 40 catches to 24 to 46 at Tennessee suggests his role fluctuated based on game plan and quarterback preference. In the NFL, that inconsistency could persist unless he develops enough route diversity to earn a stable role.

The expanding route tree is the upside signal. If he continues adding intermediate routes and becomes a more complete receiver, his volume could stabilize and his PPR floor would improve. That development arc is worth monitoring.
brazzell.badgersFit:
Brazzell is a deep-ball dart throw. The 17+ YPR early in his career is legit, but the Tennessee volume was inconsistent — 24 catches in 2024 is unacceptable for a starting SEC receiver. At pick 4.83 he's fine for your bench — he'll give you 2-3 boom weeks a year and be invisible the rest. Don't rely on him as a starter.

The Badgers-specific value is the boom-week potential at a low cost. At pick 4.83, you're spending a minimal asset on a player whose best-case scenario is a WR4 with 50+ catches and 6+ TDs. That ceiling is worth chasing in the late rounds.

The risk is the volume inconsistency. If he can't establish a stable role in an NFL offense, he'll be a situational deep threat with 30 catches and no PPR value. The expanding route tree is the development to watch — if it continues, his floor rises.
brazzell.verdict: Deep-ball specialist with inconsistent volume. Boom/bust bench WR.

coleman-jonah.shortReport: The definition of a volume grinder — a running back who produces through sheer carry volume rather than explosive efficiency. Coleman's three-year arc at Arizona and Washington (147→195→203 carries, 871→1053→1128 yards) shows a back who can handle a heavy workload and find the end zone with improving consistency (7→10→11 TDs). The receiving upside is emerging (18 catches in 2025) but still modest. He's not sexy, but he's reliable — the kind of back who gives you steady points without big plays.
strengths: The workhorse volume is his defining trait and it's proven over multiple seasons. He's carried the ball 545 times in three seasons without significant injury, which answers the durability question that many college backs can't address. NFL teams value that kind of proven durability because it means they can rely on him for 15-18 carries per game without worrying about wear and tear.

The ascending touchdown production (7→10→11) shows he's improving as a finisher near the goal line. He's learning to read blocking schemes and find the soft shoulder, which is a skill that translates to short-yardage and goal-line situations at the NFL level. That TD floor gives him standalone fantasy value even in a committee.

The receiving upside is emerging — 8→15→18 catches with 114 yards in 2025. He's not a natural pass-catcher, but he's functional as a check-down option and screen receiver. If he can develop into a 25-catch back at the NFL level, he'll have PPR flex appeal that elevates him from a pure two-down grinder.
weaknesses: The efficiency is modest — 5.9→5.4→5.6 YPC across three seasons. He's not a big-play runner who creates explosive gains; he's a grinder who gets what's blocked and occasionally breaks a tackle for an extra 5 yards. That profile caps his ceiling at RB3 in fantasy because he'll never have the 50-point explosion weeks that elite backs provide.

The heavy mileage (545 career carries) is a double-edged sword. It proves his durability, but it also means there's tread on the tires. Running backs who enter the NFL with 500+ college carries have a higher attrition rate than those with lighter workloads.

The receiving volume is still too low for a true three-down profile. Eighteen catches in a season is a functional pass-catcher, not a featured receiver out of the backfield. He'll come off the field on third-and-long in the NFL, which caps his snap count and his PPR floor.
coleman-jonah.nflFit:
Early-down grinder in a committee. Best in a power-run scheme that feeds him between the tackles and uses him near the goal line. The receiving ability keeps him on the field on some third downs, but he's not a three-down back.

The power-run scheme fit is critical because Coleman's value is concentrated in volume and TDs. In a gap-scheme offense that runs between the tackles and uses him near the goal line, he'll produce 15 carries for 65 yards with a TD chance every week. In a zone-scheme offense that values burst and receiving, he'll be a limited-role backup.

The receiving growth gives him a path to more snaps. If he can develop from 18 catches to 25-30 at the NFL level, he'll stay on the field for some third downs, which increases his snap count and his PPR floor. That development is the key variable in his fantasy projection.
coleman-jonah.comp:
David Montgomery — a volume grinder with modest efficiency who scores touchdowns and produces through carry volume. Like Montgomery, Coleman's fantasy value is rooted in volume and TDs rather than explosive plays.

The Montgomery comparison is about the grinder profile specifically. Montgomery has been a productive fantasy back because he gets volume and scores TDs — he's not explosive, but he's reliable. Coleman projects to a similar arc if he lands in the right offense. The difference is that Montgomery has better receiving ability, which gives him more PPR upside.
coleman-jonah.dynastyOutlook:
RB3/4 with weekly RB2 upside if he wins a lead role. The volume is appealing but the efficiency is modest. He's a reliable floor play without the ceiling of an elite back.

The weekly profile will be steady but unspectacular. Coleman will give you 15-65-0.5 TD most weeks — a 10-point floor in standard that won't win you matchups but won't sink you either. The TD upside is the ceiling driver — in weeks where he scores, he's an RB2.

The PPR penalty is real. With only 18 catches in 2025, Coleman provides almost no receiving floor. In PPR formats, he's an RB4 unless he scores. In standard, his volume and TD floor make him a usable flex.
coleman-jonah.badgersFit:
Coleman is a volume RB who'll give you steady points without big plays. At pick 4.108 he's solid value as your RB3 — the 200+ carry workload means he'll produce even if the YPC is boring. The receiving growth (18 catches in 2025) adds PPR flex appeal. He's not sexy but he's reliable, and in dynasty, reliable RB3s have real value.

The Badgers-specific value is the floor at a discount price. At pick 4.108, you're spending a late-round pick on a player whose worst-case scenario is 150 carries and 600 yards with 5 TDs — that's a usable RB3 in standard. Most players in this range have much wider ranges of outcomes.

The ascending TD production (7→10→11) is the strongest signal. He's improving as a finisher near the goal line, which is the most translatable fantasy skill for a volume grinder.
coleman-jonah.verdict: Volume grinder with steady floor. RB3 with RB2 upside in the right role.

klubnik.shortReport:
A dual-threat Clemson quarterback whose 5 rushing touchdowns are the fantasy differentiator in this tier. Klubnik's 78-284-5TD rushing line in 2025 gives him a weekly fantasy floor that pocket passers like Beck and Allar can't match — in a bad passing week where he throws for 200 yards and one TD, those 30 rushing yards and a rushing TD elevate him to a QB2 line. His passing production in 2025 (2,943 yards, 16 TDs, 6 INTs, 63.9%) regressed from expectations, but the dual-threat profile provides insurance that pure passers lack.

The regression in passing stats is the concern that keeps him in the QB3 tier rather than climbing higher. Sixteen passing TDs on 63.9% accuracy is modest for a starter at Clemson, where the offensive talent should produce better numbers. The expectation was that Klubnik would take a leap in 2025 after showing flashes in 2024, but instead the passing production was inconsistent — he'd have 300-yard games followed by 150-yard clunkers. That inconsistency makes him a risky weekly starter whose value is floor-dependent rather than ceiling-dependent.

The 5 rushing TDs are what keep him on the fantasy radar despite the passing regression. In superflex formats, a quarterback who scores 5 times with his legs provides a weekly floor that makes him startable even when the passing is bad. That's the kind of insurance that pocket passers like Beck (1 rushing TD) and Allar simply can't provide. If the passing improves at the NFL level — and a better supporting cast could help — the combination of improved passing with the existing rushing floor could elevate him to QB2 territory.
strengths: The dual-threat profile is the fantasy hook. Five rushing touchdowns and 284 rushing yards give him a weekly floor that makes him startable even in bad passing weeks. In a week where he throws for 200 yards and one TD, the 30 rushing yards and a rushing TD elevate him to a QB1 line. That floor is what separates him from every pocket-only QB in this tier.

The improved accuracy (63.9%) suggests he's developing as a passer, not just relying on his legs. If the accuracy improvement is real, he could become a legitimate weekly QB1 in superflex formats with both rushing and passing upside.
weaknesses: The 9 INTs show he still makes bad decisions under pressure. He'll force throws into coverage, particularly when the pocket collapses and he tries to create off-schedule. Those turnovers cost fantasy points and can sink a weekly lineup.

The 284 rush yards is modest for a 'dual-threat' — he's not Jalen Hurts or Josh Allen. He's a functional scrambler who converts third downs and scores near the goal line, but he's not going to give you 50-yard rushing explosions.
klubnik.nflFit:
Dual-threat QB in a spread/RPO system. The rushing TDs make him a fantasy asset even if the passing is inconsistent. He needs a creative coordinator who uses his mobility as a weapon rather than a last resort.

The rushing TDs are the fantasy differentiator. Five rushing touchdowns in a season is a legitimate scoring contribution that provides a weekly floor pocket passers can't match. In a bad passing week where he throws for 200 yards and one TD, the 30 rushing yards and a rushing TD elevate him to a QB1 line.

The improved accuracy (63.9%) suggests he's developing as a passer, not just relying on his legs. If the accuracy improvement is real, he could become a legitimate weekly QB1 in superflex formats.
klubnik.comp:
Dak Prescott — a functional runner with above-average passing whose fantasy value is rooted in the combination of modest rushing contribution and efficient passing. Like Prescott, Klubnik's floor is higher than pure pocket passers because of his legs.

The Prescott comparison is about the dual-threat floor. Prescott has been a productive fantasy quarterback because he contributes both through the air and on the ground. Klubnik has a similar profile — the rushing TDs give him a floor that makes him startable even when the passing is inconsistent.
klubnik.dynastyOutlook:
QB3/4 in superflex. The 5 rushing TDs give him streaming value in single-QB leagues. He's the best QB3 option in this class for fantasy purposes.

The weekly profile will be more consistent than Beck or Allar because of the rushing floor. Even in bad passing weeks, the 30-50 rushing yards and occasional rushing TD provide a baseline that keeps him in QB2 range. That consistency is valuable in superflex formats.

The 9 INTs are the concern. He still makes bad decisions under pressure, which can sink individual weeks. But the rushing floor covers for those mistakes in fantasy.
klubnik.badgersFit:
Klubnik is the best QB3 option in this class for fantasy. The 5 rushing TDs give him a weekly floor that pocket passers like Beck and Allar can't match. At pick 4.110 he's worth a stash in superflex. In single-QB he's a streamer with upside — the 63.9% accuracy is encouraging and the rushing floor means he'll rarely tank your week.

The Badgers-specific value is the rushing floor at a reasonable price. At pick 4.110, you're getting a quarterback whose worst-case scenario is a streaming option with 5 rushing TDs per season. That floor is higher than any other QB in this tier.

The 9 INTs are the risk, but the rushing floor provides insurance. In weeks where the passing is bad, the rushing contribution keeps him startable. That's the kind of floor you want from a QB3 in superflex.
klubnik.verdict: Dual-threat with rushing floor. Best QB3 for fantasy in this tier.

lance.shortReport: Trey Lance's younger brother and an FCS dominator whose 27 touchdowns in three seasons at North Dakota State demand attention regardless of competition level. The 18.3 YPR as a freshman is eye-popping — it means he was averaging nearly 20 yards every time he caught the ball — and the consistent TD production (9-11 per season) shows a receiver who knows how to finish. The FCS-to-NFL jump is the obvious risk, but NDSU has a track record of producing NFL talent (Christian Watson, Carson Wentz), and the program's pro-style offense means Lance is more prepared for NFL route concepts than most small-school prospects.
strengths: The touchdown production is the hook and it's rooted in a specific skill: finishing at the catch point. Lance doesn't just catch the ball in the end zone — he tracks it early, adjusts his body position, and secures it with strong hands that refuse to let defenders rip it out. That finishing ability produced 27 TDs in three seasons at a level where defenses knew he was the primary target. The skill translates because it's rooted in hand strength and spatial awareness, not just athletic dominance over lesser defenders.

The elite YPR (18.3→15.8→15.8) reflects genuine deep-ball ability rooted in ball-tracking and body control. He doesn't win with pure speed — he wins by adjusting to the ball in flight and making the catch at the high point. That's a translatable skill because it works against any level of defender; the ball doesn't change speed based on the competition.

The NDSU pedigree matters more than most evaluators realize. The Bison run a pro-style offense with play-action concepts, zone-run schemes, and progression-read passing that prepares receivers for NFL route concepts. Lance isn't coming from a spread system where he ran three routes — he's been coached in a system that translates to the next level.
weaknesses: The FCS competition is the obvious and legitimate concern. The defensive backs he faced at NDSU aren't NFL-caliber — they're smaller, slower, and less technically sound than what he'll see on Sundays. The best-case scenario is that his ball-tracking and finishing skills translate because they're technique-driven; the worst-case scenario is that he can't separate against corners who can match his physical profile.

The TD rate will regress dramatically. Eleven touchdowns on 33 catches as a freshman is a 33% rate — nearly triple the NFL average. Even at NDSU, that rate came down to 13.5% by 2025. Expect 5-7 TDs per NFL season, which is productive but not elite.

The route tree is likely limited by the NDSU offense, which is run-heavy with play-action shots. He's been running go-balls, posts, and seam routes — the deep stuff that works when defenses are loading the box to stop the run. The question is whether he can run the full tree with the precision NFL offenses demand.
lance.nflFit:
Deep-ball Z-receiver in a vertical offense that takes shots downfield. The FCS jump is real but NDSU has a track record. Best case: he's a Christian Watson-type contributor. Worst case: he can't separate and becomes a practice-squad player.

The NDSU pedigree is a legitimate signal. The Bison run a pro-style offense with play-action concepts that prepare receivers for NFL route concepts. Lance isn't coming from a spread system where he ran three routes — he's been coached in a system that translates to the next level. That preparation gives him a higher floor than most FCS prospects.

The deep-ball tracking is his most translatable skill. He adjusts to the ball in flight with natural hands and body control, which means he'll catch deep balls even when the throw isn't perfect. That skill works against any level of defender because the ball doesn't change speed based on the competition.
lance.comp:
Christian Watson — another NDSU deep-ball specialist whose athletic upside translated to a productive NFL role. Lance doesn't have Watson's elite straight-line speed, but the program pedigree and production profile are similar.

The Watson comparison is about the NDSU-to-NFL arc specifically. Watson was a second-round pick who produced immediately because the NDSU offense prepared him for NFL concepts. Lance has a similar preparation advantage, though he lacks Watson's elite speed, which caps his ceiling as a vertical threat.
lance.dynastyOutlook:
WR5/6 with WR3 upside if he translates. The FCS jump is the risk; the TD production and NDSU pedigree are the reward. He's a taxi squad stash with legitimate upside.

The range of outcomes is extremely wide. Best case: he lands in a vertical offense and produces 45-700-7, making him a WR4 in TD-heavy formats. Worst case: he can't separate against NFL corners and becomes a practice-squad player. That variance makes him a polarizing pick.

The TD production is the hook. Twenty-seven touchdowns in three seasons is historic at any level. Even with regression to 5-7 TDs per NFL season, that's a usable floor in TD-heavy formats where most WR5/6 types don't score at all.
lance.badgersFit:
Lance is my favorite deep-league stash. 27 TDs at NDSU is no joke — that program produces NFL players consistently. At pick 4.136 he's a free dart throw. The 18+ YPR means he's a big-play threat whenever he's on the field. The FCS jump is real but NDSU WRs have hit before. Stash him on your taxi squad and pray — this is the kind of pick that wins championships when it hits.

The Badgers-specific value is the upside at a minimal cost. At pick 4.136, you're spending a late-round pick on a player whose best-case scenario is a WR3 with 50+ catches and 7+ TDs. That ceiling is worth chasing when the cost is this low.

The NDSU pedigree is the differentiator. Christian Watson, Carson Wentz, and multiple other NDSU products have translated to the NFL. The program's pro-style offense prepares players for the next level better than most FCS programs. That preparation gives Lance a higher floor than his competition level would suggest.
lance.verdict: FCS TD machine with deep-ball ability. Late-round stash with upside.

morton.shortReport: An Air Raid product whose quick release and accuracy in the short-to-intermediate game are offset by limited arm strength and minimal rushing value. Morton's 2,944-21-9 line at Texas Tech looks productive until you account for the system — the Red Raiders throw the ball 50+ times per game, which inflates quarterback numbers dramatically. His 62 rush yards and 1 TD give him no fantasy floor, and the arm strength limitations cap his passing ceiling.
strengths: The quick release is his most translatable skill. He gets the ball out fast with clean mechanics, which allows him to operate efficiently in timing-based passing games. That quick release will get him a look as a backup in a West Coast system.
weaknesses: The limited arm strength prevents him from driving the ball on deep outs and vertical concepts. He's a dink-and-dunk quarterback whose production is dependent on the system creating open receivers underneath. In the NFL, where windows are tighter and defenders close faster, that limitation caps his ceiling.

The minimal rushing (62 yards, 1 TD) gives him no fantasy floor. He's a pure pocket passer whose value is entirely dependent on his passing production — and that production is inflated by the Air Raid system.
morton.nflFit:
Backup QB in a West Coast system. The quick release is his calling card but the arm strength limits his ceiling to spot starts and relief duty.

The quick release is his most translatable skill. He gets the ball out fast with clean mechanics, which allows him to operate efficiently in timing-based passing games. That skill will get him a look as a backup in a West Coast system.

The Air Raid inflation is the concern. Texas Tech throws the ball 50+ times per game, which inflates quarterback numbers dramatically. Morton's production is a product of the system, not his individual ability.
morton.comp:
Cooper Rush — quick-release backup with limited upside. Like Rush, Morton can manage a game but can't create outside of structure.

The Rush comparison is about the backup-with-quick-release profile. Rush has survived in the NFL because he can manage a game efficiently, but he can't create outside of structure. Morton has a similar profile with the added concern of Air Raid system inflation.
morton.dynastyOutlook:
QB7. Not rosterable in any format.

The minimal rushing (62 yards, 1 TD) gives him no fantasy floor. He's a pure pocket passer whose value is entirely dependent on his passing production — and that production is inflated by the Air Raid system.

The limited arm strength prevents him from driving the ball on deep outs and vertical concepts. He's a dink-and-dunk quarterback whose production is dependent on the system creating open receivers underneath.
morton.badgersFit:
Morton is off the fantasy radar. 62 rush yards and limited arm strength make him a backup-only prospect whose production is inflated by the Air Raid system. At pick 7.234 he's a hard pass for fantasy.

The Badgers-specific concern is the Air Raid inflation. Texas Tech's system creates volume that won't exist in most NFL offenses. Morton's numbers are a product of the system, not his individual ability.

The lack of rushing floor and limited arm strength make him unrosterable in any dynasty format. There's no fantasy skill here to build around.

nussmeier.shortReport: An LSU gunslinger whose arm talent and 24-TD production are tantalizing, but whose 11 INTs and minimal rushing value (28 yards, 1 TD) make him a boom/bust streamer at best. Nussmeier can win you a week with 350 yards and 3 TDs, then lose you the next with 200 yards and 3 INTs. The high variance is his identity — it's not a bug, it's a feature of his gunslinger mentality. In superflex, he's a streaming option during bye weeks. In single-QB, he's unrosterable.
strengths: The arm talent is elite and it shows up on every type of throw — deep balls, back-shoulder fades, seam routes into tight windows. That arm talent gives him a passing ceiling that most quarterbacks in this tier can't match, and it's what makes him a streaming option in superflex formats.
The 24 TDs and 3,112 yards show he can produce in volume when the game script favors passing. In the right matchup — trailing against a soft secondary — he can explode for a QB1 week.
weaknesses: The 11 INTs reflect a gunslinger mentality that creates as many negative plays as positive ones. He'll force throws into coverage, particularly when he's trying to make something happen in a bad game script. Those turnovers cost fantasy points and can sink a weekly lineup.

The minimal rushing (28 yards, 1 TD) gives him almost no fantasy floor. In a bad passing week, he'll give you a QB3 line with nothing to fall back on. That floor limitation makes him unstartable in single-QB leagues.
nussmeier.nflFit:
Backup QB with starter upside in the right system. Best in a vertical passing game that maximizes arm talent and tolerates occasional turnovers.

The arm talent is the selling point. He can make every type of throw — deep balls, back-shoulder fades, seam routes into tight windows — which gives him a passing ceiling that most quarterbacks in this tier can't match.

The 11 INTs reflect a gunslinger mentality that creates as many negative plays as positive ones. He'll force throws into coverage, particularly when he's trying to make something happen in a bad game script. Those turnovers cost fantasy points and can sink a weekly lineup.
nussmeier.comp:
Jameis Winston — a gunslinger with high variance whose brilliant weeks are canceled out by terrible ones. Like Winston, Nussmeier's fantasy value is concentrated in boom/bust streaming.

The Winston comparison is about the gunslinger profile specifically. Winston has had some of the best and worst fantasy weeks of any quarterback in the NFL. Nussmeier has a similar variance — he can win you a week with 350 yards and 3 TDs, then lose you the next with 200 yards and 3 INTs.
nussmeier.dynastyOutlook:
QB5/6 in superflex. Streaming option with boom/bust weeks. Not rosterable in single-QB.

The weekly variance is extreme. Nussmeier will give you 2-3 boom weeks per season where he throws for 300+ yards and 3+ TDs, and 5-6 bust weeks where he throws multiple INTs. That variance makes him a better best-ball pick than a weekly-lineup asset.

The minimal rushing (28 yards, 1 TD) gives him almost no fantasy floor. In a bad passing week, he'll give you a QB3 line with nothing to fall back on.
nussmeier.badgersFit:
Nussmeier is a boom/bust streamer in superflex. 24 TDs is nice but 11 INTs and 28 rush yards make him unrosterable in single-QB. At pick 7.249 he's a free dart in 2QB leagues only. The LSU pedigree gives him a chance, but the gunslinger mentality will kill your week as often as he wins it.

The Badgers-specific concern is the variance. In dynasty, you need quarterbacks who produce consistently, not ones who win you one week and lose you the next. Nussmeier's gunslinger mentality makes him unrosterable in single-QB and a risky streamer in superflex.

The arm talent is real but it's not enough without the floor. At pick 7.249, you're spending a last-round pick on a player whose ceiling is a streaming QB in superflex. That's not enough value to justify a roster spot.
nussmeier.verdict: LSU gunslinger. Boom/bust superflex streamer only.

stowers.shortReport:
The TE2 in this class — and the Mackey Award winner — whose ascending production at Vanderbilt (50→62 catches across 2024-25) earned him first-team All-American honors in 2025. Stowers transferred from Texas A&M to Vanderbilt and immediately became one of the most productive tight ends in the SEC, creating mismatches in the slot with his combination of size and athleticism that defensive coordinators couldn't solve. He's not as raw-athletically dynamic as Sadiq, but he's a more proven producer with a higher floor and a Mackey Award to validate it.

What separates Stowers from the other tight ends in this class is the consistency of his production in an unfavorable offensive environment.
