The knee injury history is the disqualifying concern for long-term dynasty value. Even if the production is ascending, the durability risk makes him a risky hold beyond 1-2 years.
raridon.badgersFit:
Raridon is a streaming TE with a safe floor. 36-412-4 at ND is solid but the knee history is a real concern for long-term durability. At pick 4.95 he's worth a look in deep TE-premium leagues. In standard he's a streamer. The ascending production (21→36 catches) is encouraging.

The Badgers-specific value is the ascending production at a reasonable price. At pick 4.95, you're getting a player whose production is trending upward, which suggests he hasn't peaked yet.

The knee history is the risk. In dynasty, durability is a major factor in long-term value. If Raridon's knees don't hold up, his NFL career could be short.
raridon.verdict: Steady possession TE. Streamer with safe floor.

hibner.shortReport:
An athletic but unproven tight end who couldn't produce at Michigan and managed just 18-192-2TD at SMU after transferring. The Michigan pedigree suggests he has the athletic profile to compete at the highest level — elite programs don't offer scholarships to players who can't play — but the inability to earn a role at Michigan and the modest production at SMU paint a concerning picture of a player whose athletic tools haven't translated to football production.

The Michigan-to-SMU trajectory is the opposite of what you want to see in a dynasty prospect. At Michigan, he was behind multiple tight ends on the depth chart and couldn't crack the rotation despite being recruited as a high-level athlete. At SMU, where the competition was less intense, he produced just 18 catches for 192 yards — numbers that are underwhelming even for a developmental tight end at the G5 level. The two-program failure suggests his football skills (route-running, blocking technique, understanding of NFL concepts) haven't developed at the rate his physical tools would predict.
strengths: The Michigan pedigree suggests he has the athletic profile to compete at the highest level — he was recruited by an elite program that doesn't offer scholarships to players who can't play.
weaknesses: Couldn't produce at Michigan and managed only 18-192-2 at SMU — a trajectory that suggests his talent level doesn't match his athletic profile. The inability to capitalize on opportunity at either stop is a red flag.
hibner.nflFit:
Developmental TE. Practice squad candidate whose value is limited to athletic potential rather than proven production.

The Michigan pedigree suggests he has the athletic profile to compete at the highest level, but the inability to earn a role at an elite program and the modest G5 production don't project to fantasy relevance.

The 18-192-2 at SMU is replacement-level production that doesn't suggest any translatable receiving skill.
hibner.comp:
Practice squad TE — an athlete who hasn't translated his physical tools into football production.

The practice-squad comparison is about the unproven athletic profile. Hibner has the physical tools but hasn't produced at any stop, which suggests his football skills don't match his athletic profile.
hibner.dynastyOutlook:
Not rosterable.

The inability to produce at Michigan and the modest SMU production don't project to any fantasy value. He's an athlete who hasn't translated his physical tools into football production.

The Michigan pedigree is the only positive signal, but it's not enough without production to back it up.
hibner.badgersFit:
Hibner is off the dynasty radar. 18-192-2 at SMU is replacement-level production from a player who couldn't produce at Michigan. At pick 5.133 he's a hard pass.

The Badgers-specific concern is the two-program failure. If Hibner couldn't produce at Michigan or SMU, he's unlikely to produce in the NFL where the competition is even tougher.

The Michigan pedigree is the only positive, but it's not enough to justify a roster spot without production to back it up.

joly.shortReport:
The best TE3 option in this class — a productive move tight end whose ascending production at NC State (40-482-5TD in 2024, 55-656-6TD in 2025) after transferring from UConn proves his skills translate across competition levels. Joly isn't a dynamic athlete who creates mismatches like Sadiq or Stowers, but he's a reliable chain-mover whose 55 catches in 2025 give him a PPR floor that most tight ends in this class can't match.

The UConn-to-NC State translation is the strongest signal in his profile and it's one that evaluators should weigh heavily. He produced at the G5 level (UConn) and then proved those skills were real by producing even more at the P5 level (NC State). That translation across competition levels is the closest thing you get to a guarantee that his skills are real — route-running, reliable hands, and the ability to find the soft spot in zone coverage are technique-driven abilities that don't disappear against better competition. The ascending trend (40→55 catches, 5→6 TDs) shows he's still improving, which gives him a higher floor than most TEs in this range.

The lack of explosiveness is the ceiling cap that keeps him from being a TE1 in any format. His 12.1-11.9 YPR shows he wins through positioning and reliability rather than athletic ability — he's not going to run by linebackers or high-point balls over safeties. That profile translates to a long NFL career as a TE2 who catches 45-55 balls per season, but his fantasy ceiling is capped at streaming in standard leagues and a viable TE2 in TE-premium formats.
strengths: The ascending production (40→55 catches, 5→6 TDs) shows he's earning a larger role rather than just benefiting from opportunity. That development arc is the strongest signal in his profile and suggests he'll continue to improve at the NFL level.

The G5-to-P5 translation (UConn to NC State) is a positive signal — he produced at both stops, which suggests his skills are real and not just a product of weaker competition. The 55 catches in 2025 is real volume for a tight end that gives him a PPR floor.
The move TE profile allows him to create favorable matchups in the slot, which generates targets in the intermediate areas of the field where tight ends produce most efficiently.
weaknesses: He's not a dynamic athlete who creates mismatches. His 12.1-11.9 YPR shows he wins through positioning and reliability rather than explosiveness, which caps his ceiling at every level. He'll never be a weekly TE1 because he doesn't have the athletic profile to generate explosive plays.
The TD rate (5-6 on 40-55 catches) is modest for a tight end with his volume. He's a chain-mover who occasionally finds the end zone, not a red-zone weapon who scores consistently.
joly.nflFit:
Move TE in a spread offense. Best as a TE2 who creates mismatches in the slot and moves the chains. He'll have a productive NFL career but a limited fantasy one.

The move TE profile allows him to create favorable matchups in the slot, which generates targets in the intermediate areas of the field where tight ends produce most efficiently.

The G5-to-P5 translation (UConn to NC State) is a positive signal — he produced at both stops, which suggests his skills are real.
joly.comp:
Dalton Schultz — productive move TE without elite athleticism whose fantasy value is rooted in volume and reliability. Like Schultz, Joly will give you consistent production without the boom weeks.

The Schultz comparison is about the productive-move-TE-without-elite-athleticism profile. Schultz has been a productive fantasy tight end because he gets volume and catches the ball reliably, but he doesn't have the athletic profile to generate explosive plays. Joly has a similar profile.
joly.dynastyOutlook:
TE3. Streaming option with TE2 upside in the right offense. The ascending production and G5-to-P5 translation are encouraging signals.

The weekly profile will be volume-dependent. In weeks where the game script favors the tight end, Joly can produce TE2 numbers. In weeks where the offense runs through the wide receivers, he'll give you 4-45-0, which is a TE3 line.

The ascending production (40→55 catches) suggests he's still improving, which gives him a higher floor than most TEs in this range.
joly.badgersFit:
Joly is the best TE3 option in this class. 55-656-6 at NC State is solid production with an ascending trend that suggests he's still improving. At pick 5.152 he's a steal if you need TE depth. In TE-premium leagues he's a viable TE2. In standard he's a streamer with upside. The 55 catches give him a PPR floor most TEs in this class can't match.

The Badgers-specific value is the ascending production at a low cost. At pick 5.152, you're getting a player whose production is trending upward and whose G5-to-P5 translation suggests real skills. That floor is higher than most TEs in this range.

The risk is the lack of explosiveness. His 12.1-11.9 YPR shows he wins through positioning and reliability rather than athletic ability, which caps his ceiling at TE2 in the best case.
joly.verdict: Productive move TE. Best TE3 in this class with TE2 upside.

koziol.shortReport: A blocking-only tight end whose 14-112-1TD at Houston after transferring from Ball State is replacement-level production. He'll stick on an NFL roster as a blocker but won't produce fantasy points.

nowakowski.shortReport: A fullback/H-back hybrid whose 11-89-0TD at Indiana after transferring from Wisconsin is negligible production. He's a blocking specialist who contributes as a lead blocker and special-teamer — not a fantasy asset.

royer.shortReport: A receiving-only tight end whose 22-248-2TD at Cincinnati after transferring from Ohio State is modest production. He contributes as a pass-catcher but doesn't block, which limits his NFL role to specific packages. Deep-league streamer at best.
royer.verdict: Receiving-only TE. Deep-league streamer at best.

cuevas.shortReport: A blocking specialist whose 8-64-0TD at Alabama after transferring from Cal Poly is negligible production. He earned a roster spot at an elite program through blocking, not receiving. No fantasy value.

traore.shortReport: An international prospect whose 10-88-1TD at Mississippi State reflects his raw developmental state. Traore has athletic upside but is years away from contributing at the NFL level. Taxi squad stash only in the deepest of leagues.
traore.verdict: Raw international TE. Taxi squad stash only.

sharp.shortReport: A blocking/inline tight end whose 15-134-1TD at LSU after transferring from Oklahoma is negligible production. He contributes as a blocker and special-teamer, not as a receiver. No fantasy value.

endries.shortReport: A possession tight end whose 20-198-2TD at Texas after transferring from Cal is modest production that doesn't project to fantasy relevance. He catches the ball reliably but has no seam-stretching ability and minimal scoring upside. Deep-league streamer at best.
endries.verdict: Possession TE. Deep-league streamer at best.

kanak.shortReport: A former linebacker convert whose 6-48-0TD at Oklahoma reflects his developmental state as a tight end. He contributes on special teams but hasn't developed as a receiver. No fantasy value.

ryan.shortReport: A blocking tight end whose 9-72-1TD at BYU after transferring from UCLA is negligible production. He contributes as a blocker, not a receiver. No fantasy value.

bentley.shortReport: A developmental tight end whose 4-28-0TD at Utah after transferring from Snow College reflects his raw state. He's years away from contributing and off the dynasty radar.

bredeson.shortReport:
A traditional fullback whose 18-62-2TD rushing and 8-52-1TD receiving at Michigan reflect his role as a lead blocker and occasional short-yardage option. Bredeson is the rare true fullback in this draft class — a player whose value is built on lead blocking, special teams, and short-yardage contributions rather than receiving or rushing production. He'll have an NFL career, but not a fantasy one.

The Michigan pedigree validates his blocking ability. The Wolverines run one of the most physical ground games in college football, and Bredeson earned significant snaps as the lead blocker in that system. NFL teams that run power concepts — Baltimore, San Francisco, Detroit — will covet a fullback who can create running lanes and contribute on special teams. That role guarantees him an NFL paycheck but produces zero fantasy points in standard formats.

The 3 total touchdowns (2 rushing, 1 receiving) are the only fantasy-adjacent production in his profile, and they came on designed short-yardage plays near the goal line. Even in the best-case scenario where an NFL team uses him as a goal-line option, he's scoring 1-2 TDs per season — not enough to justify a roster spot in any fantasy format except leagues with specific fullback scoring.
bredeson.badgersFit: Bredeson is a real football player but not a fantasy asset. Fullbacks don't produce in standard formats — they block, they play special teams, and they occasionally catch a check-down. At pick 7.159 he's undraftable for fantasy purposes. Only relevant in leagues with specific FB scoring.
bredeson.verdict: Traditional FB. No standard fantasy value.

smack.shortReport: The only kicker in this class worth drafting, and even then only if your league starts a K. Smack's 22-27 FG (81.5%) with a 56-yard long and 48-48 XP in 2025 is NFL-caliber accuracy and range. The 82.4% touchback rate on kickoffs confirms the leg strength. But kicker fantasy value is almost entirely landing-spot dependent — a good offense makes him a K1, a bad offense makes him unrosterable.
strengths: The 81.5% FG accuracy with a 56-yard long is NFL-caliber. He's reliable from mid-range and has the leg strength to connect from 50+, which gives him a weekly ceiling that weaker-legged kickers can't match.
The 48-48 XP and 82.4% touchback rate show he's consistent and has the leg strength to handle NFL kickoff duties, which gives him a roster floor.
weaknesses: Five missed field goals isn't terrible, but it shows he's not automatic — he'll have weeks where a missed kick costs you fantasy points.
Kicker value is almost entirely landing-spot dependent. A bad offense means fewer PAT attempts and fewer FG opportunities, which directly reduces his fantasy production regardless of his accuracy.
smack.nflFit: NFL kicker. Will get drafted based on leg strength and accuracy. Landing spot determines his fantasy value entirely.
smack.comp: Jake Moody — reliable college kicker with NFL range whose fantasy value was determined entirely by his landing spot in San Francisco.
smack.dynastyOutlook: K2. Draft him if your league starts a K and you want a rookie. Landing spot determines his value entirely.
smack.badgersFit: Smack is the only K worth drafting in this class. 81.5% with a 56-yard long is NFL-caliber accuracy and range. At pick 7.216 he's worth a last-round pick if your league starts a K. His value is 100% landing-spot dependent — a good offense makes him a K1, a bad offense makes him unrosterable.
smack.verdict: Reliable rookie K. Landing-spot dependent value.

sarratt.shortReport:
One of the most accomplished route-runners outside the top tier, Sarratt's four-year production arc across three programs — Saint Francis, James Madison, and Indiana — is a masterclass in translating talent across competition levels. The 82-1191-8TD explosion at JMU in 2023 put him on the map, and instead of resting on a G5 resume, he proved it again against Big Ten defenses with 53-957-8TD in 2024 and 65-830-15TD in 2025. Fifteen touchdowns in his final season — second-team All-Big Ten — tells you he was one of the best receivers in the conference against the best cornerbacks.

What makes Sarratt different from other multi-transfer receivers is the consistency of his production regardless of scheme, quarterback, or conference. At Saint Francis he produced against NEC defenses. At JMU he produced against Sun Belt defenses. At Indiana he produced against Big Ten defenses. The common variable across all three stops is Sarratt himself — his route-running, his hands, and his football IQ are the constants that generated production in every environment. That kind of scheme-proof reliability is rare and it's exactly what NFL scouts look for.

The 15-touchdown spike in 2025 is the headline, but the underlying production is what matters for dynasty. He caught 65 balls for 830 yards in a balanced Indiana offense that featured multiple weapons — he wasn't force-fed targets, he earned them. The 12.8 YPR shows he's winning at all three levels of the field, not just underneath. The risk is the age — as a super-senior, he'll be 24 as a rookie — but the production arc suggests his skills are real and sustainable.
strengths: The production across three competition levels (NEC, Sun Belt, Big Ten) is the strongest signal in his profile. He didn't just produce at the G5 level and hope it translated — he proved it at every stop, including against Big Ten defenses in 2024-25. That translation is the closest thing you get to a guarantee that his skills are real.

The 15-touchdown season in 2025 reflects genuine red-zone ability. He finds the soft spot in zone coverage, presents a clean target, and finishes with strong hands. That skill translates to the NFL because it's rooted in spatial awareness and body control, not just athletic dominance.

The route-running is polished for a player who spent his early years at lower-level programs. He runs sharp intermediate routes — curls, comebacks, digs — with the kind of precision that creates separation against man coverage. That polish gives him a PPR floor because he converts targets into catches at a high rate.
weaknesses: The age is a dynasty concern. As a super-senior who played at three programs, he'll be 24 as a rookie, which limits his developmental upside and makes him a win-now asset rather than a keeper piece.

The YPR decline from 18.1 (2024) to 12.8 (2025) reflects increased volume but also a shift toward shorter routes. He's becoming more of a possession receiver and less of a deep-ball threat, which caps his standard-scoring ceiling.
The Indiana spread system created schemed openings that inflated some of his production. NFL defenses will take away the easy completions and force him to win against tighter coverage.
sarratt.nflFit:
Possession Z-receiver in a timing-based offense. The multi-program translation proves his skills are real. Best in a system that values intermediate route-running and red-zone reliability.

The scheme-proof nature of his game is what makes him a safe NFL projection. He's produced at three programs, with three different quarterbacks, in three different conferences. That consistency suggests his production is player-driven, not system-driven.

The age limits his developmental window. He's entering the NFL closer to his finished product than most rookies, which means what you see is what you get — and what you see is a reliable WR3 with TD upside.
sarratt.comp:
Adam Thielen — a multi-stop receiver who proved himself at every level through route-running and reliability rather than elite athleticism. Like Thielen, Sarratt's value is in his consistency and his ability to produce regardless of his environment.

The Thielen comparison is about the self-made profile. Thielen was an undrafted free agent who became a Pro Bowl receiver through route-running and determination. Sarratt has a similar arc — he climbed from Saint Francis to JMU to Indiana, producing at every stop.
sarratt.dynastyOutlook:
WR4/5 with weekly WR3 upside. The multi-program translation is the strongest signal in his profile, but the age caps his long-term value. He's a win-now asset rather than a keeper.

The floor is his best attribute. Sarratt will catch 55-65 balls per season because he's always open by enough and he always catches the ball. That floor gives him a weekly PPR baseline of 8-10 points, which is a usable flex play.

The 15-TD ceiling won't repeat in the NFL — expect 6-8 TDs as a realistic projection. But even with TD regression, 60 catches and 750 yards with 7 TDs is a solid WR3 in PPR.
sarratt.badgersFit:
Sarratt is a reliable WR3 whose multi-program production proves his skills are real. At pick 3.115 he's excellent value — 15 TDs at Indiana is elite, and the Big Ten production against real defenses translates. The age (super-senior) limits his dynasty ceiling, but the floor is high enough to justify the pick. Draft him as your WR3/4 and enjoy the consistency.

The Badgers-specific value is the proven translation. He's produced at three programs across three conferences — that's as close to a guarantee as you'll find in dynasty. At pick 3.115, you're getting a player whose floor is a productive WR4 in PPR.

The TD regression is coming — 15 TDs won't repeat in the NFL. But even at 6-8 TDs with 60 catches, he's a usable flex play every week.
sarratt.verdict: Multi-program producer with proven translation. Reliable WR3 with TD upside.

bell-chris.shortReport:
A big outside receiver whose 2025 breakout at Louisville (72-917-6TD in 11 games before a torn ACL) was the most exciting development for any receiver in this tier — until the knee gave out. Bell was on pace for 100+ catches and 1,200+ yards before the injury ended his season in game 11, and the tape from those 11 games shows a receiver who wins with size, physicality, and contested-catch ability at every level of the field. The ACL is the obvious risk, but the pre-injury production is undeniable.

The contested-catch ability is what separates Bell from other big receivers in this class. He doesn't just rely on his size to win jump balls — he uses body positioning, hand strength, and timing to create a catching zone that most corners can't contest. His 17.1 YPR in 2024 reflects genuine deep-ball ability, and while the YPR dropped to 12.7 in 2025 as his role expanded, that decline is a positive signal — it means he was winning on intermediate routes too, not just deep balls.

The ACL tear is the swing factor for his entire dynasty evaluation. If he recovers fully and the explosiveness returns, he's a WR3 with WR2 upside whose size and physicality give him a defined NFL role. If the recovery is slow or the burst doesn't come back, he's a camp body whose promising tape becomes irrelevant. The medical evaluation will determine everything.
strengths: The pre-injury production (72-917-6TD in 11 games) projects to 105-1335-9TD over a full season — that's WR1 volume at Louisville. The pace tells you he was the focal point of the offense and producing at an elite rate.

The contested-catch ability gives him a defined NFL role regardless of scheme. Big receivers who win at the catch point always have a place in the NFL because the skill is quarterback-friendly — the passer doesn't need a perfect throw, he just needs to give Bell a chance.

The YPR evolution (17.1→12.7) shows he's developing from a deep-ball specialist into a more complete receiver. He's running intermediate routes and producing underneath, which expands his role beyond just vertical shots.
weaknesses: The torn ACL is the defining risk. Recovery timelines for ACL tears are typically 9-12 months, and receivers who rely on explosiveness often take 18+ months to regain their pre-injury level. Bell may not be fully healthy for his rookie season.
The one-year breakout concern applies — his 2024 season (43-737-4TD) was modest, and the 2025 explosion could represent a system or opportunity peak rather than sustainable development.
The ACL tear specifically limits his ability to win on the contested catches that define his game — those catches require explosive leaping and body control that may be compromised during recovery.
bell-chris.nflFit:
X-receiver in a vertical offense. The contested-catch ability gives him a defined role if healthy. The ACL recovery timeline is the swing factor.

The size and physicality make him a natural fit as a boundary receiver who wins on 50/50 balls, red-zone fades, and back-shoulder throws. That role exists on every NFL team.

The ACL recovery will determine his rookie-year opportunity. If he's fully healthy by training camp, he can compete for a starting role immediately. If he's not, he may redshirt his first season.
bell-chris.comp:
Allen Lazard — a big, physical receiver who wins at the catch point and produces through physicality rather than separation. Like Lazard, Bell's value is in his size and his hands, not his speed or route-running.

The Lazard comparison is about the big-body X-receiver profile. Lazard has produced in the NFL because he wins contested catches and contributes in the red zone. Bell has a similar profile with a higher ceiling if healthy.
bell-chris.dynastyOutlook:
WR4/5 with WR2 upside if the ACL recovery goes well. The pre-injury production is tantalizing but the knee is the swing factor. He's a buy-low candidate whose value depends entirely on medical reports.

The range of outcomes is extremely wide. Best case: he recovers fully and produces 70+ catches with 6+ TDs per season — a WR3 in PPR. Worst case: the ACL recovery stalls and he can't regain his pre-injury explosiveness.

The buy-low window is the dynasty opportunity. If you can acquire Bell at a discount because of the ACL, you're getting a player whose pre-injury tape projects to WR2 production.
bell-chris.badgersFit:
Bell is the best buy-low in this class. 72-917-6TD in 11 games before the ACL tear is elite production pace that you can acquire at a discount. At pick 5.94 the risk is priced in — if the knee recovers, you've got a WR3 with WR2 upside. If it doesn't, you've lost a fifth-round pick. The contested-catch skill is the separator.

The ACL is the swing factor. If you're comfortable with the medical risk, Bell is the best value in this tier. The pre-injury production was legitimate and the skill set is NFL-ready.

The risk is real — ACL recoveries for explosive receivers are unpredictable. But at pick 5.94, the reward far outweighs the cost.
bell-chris.verdict: ACL risk with elite pre-injury production. Buy-low WR with big upside.

thomas.shortReport:
A slot/return hybrid whose transfer from Mississippi State to LSU in 2025 produced a modest but useful stat line (45-612-4TD, 13.6 YPR) with punt-return contributions. Thomas wins in the slot with quickness and short-area burst — he's the kind of receiver who turns 5-yard slants into 15-yard gains because the first defender rarely tackles him cleanly. The LSU production against SEC defenses validates that his skills are real, not just a product of inferior competition at Mississippi State.

The slot profile is both his strength and his limitation. He's excellent in a defined role — quick routes, schemed touches, motion-based concepts — but he can't win on the boundary against physical press corners and he doesn't have the deep speed to stress defenses vertically. His fantasy ceiling is capped by that slot-only limitation, but his floor is solid because slot receivers always have a role in modern offenses.

The punt-return ability adds a dimension of value that most slot receivers in this class lack. In return-yard leagues, Thomas has legitimate WR5 value because the return production adds 2-3 points per week on top of his receiving contributions. In standard formats, he's a bench stash whose 45-catch volume gives him a tiny PPR floor.
strengths: The SEC production at LSU validates his skills against elite competition. Forty-five catches for 612 yards in the SEC slot is solid — he earned targets against defensive backs who will be in the NFL.
The short-area quickness is his most translatable skill. He creates separation on quick routes with sharp breaks and sudden direction changes that NFL corners will struggle to mirror in the slot.
The punt-return ability gives him a roster floor that insulates against the worst-case receiving scenario.
weaknesses: Slot-only profile limits his snap count to roughly 60% of offensive plays, which caps his volume and his fantasy ceiling.
The 45-catch volume is modest and may represent his ceiling rather than a stepping stone to more targets.
The 13.6 YPR shows he wins underneath rather than downfield, which caps his standard-scoring value.
thomas.nflFit:
Slot receiver and punt returner in a spread offense. Best as a WR4/5 who contributes on special teams and provides schemed touches from the slot.

The slot quickness is his NFL skill. He runs option routes, angle routes, and crossers with the kind of sharp breaks that create separation against man coverage. A creative coordinator will use motion and schemed touches to get him the ball in space.

The punt-return ability gives him a roster floor. Even if the offense doesn't feature him, he'll contribute on special teams.
thomas.comp:
Nico Collins early career — a slot/return contributor who found a role through quickness and special teams before developing into a more complete receiver. Thomas has a similar path if his route tree expands.

The Collins comparison is about the developmental path, not the physical profile. Collins found his NFL footing through slot work and special teams before becoming a featured receiver. Thomas could follow a similar arc.
thomas.dynastyOutlook:
WR5/6 with return-league value. The slot production is solid but the volume ceiling is modest. He's a bench stash with PPR flex appeal in deep leagues.

The PPR floor is his best attribute. Forty-five catches gives him a weekly baseline of 4-5 points, which is usable as a WR5/6 in deep PPR formats.

The return value adds 2-3 points per week in return-yard leagues, which elevates him to a viable WR4/5.
thomas.badgersFit:
Thomas is a slot/return depth piece with SEC credentials. 45-612-4 at LSU is solid production against real defenses. At pick 5.89 he's worth a bench spot in PPR and return-yard leagues. The quickness and return ability give him a defined NFL role.

The Badgers-specific value is the SEC validation at a low cost. At pick 5.89, you're getting a player whose production came against elite competition.

The slot-only limitation caps his ceiling but the return value gives him a roster floor.
thomas.verdict: Slot/returner with SEC credentials. PPR depth with return-league value.

black.shortReport:
The most intriguing running back story in this class — a JMU transfer who became a key contributor on Indiana's national championship team with 1,040 rushing yards and 10 touchdowns in 2025. Black's emergence was one of the biggest surprises of the college season, going from a 251-yard backup role in 2024 to the lead back on the best team in the country. The 5.6 YPC shows he was efficient, not just a volume beneficiary, and the 28-214-2TD receiving line adds a PPR dimension that most late-round backs can't match.

What makes Black's profile interesting for dynasty is the context of the production. He wasn't handed the starting job — he earned it on a roster loaded with talent, on a team that won the national championship. That tells you the coaches trusted him more than the alternatives, which is a signal that his skills are real even if the sample is limited. The 28 catches are particularly encouraging because they suggest he's not just a two-down grinder — he has enough pass-catching ability to stay on the field in passing situations.

The one-year breakout is the obvious risk. Going from 251 yards as a backup to 1,040 as a starter could represent genuine development, or it could represent a system and opportunity peak that won't repeat in the NFL. The Indiana offensive line was excellent in 2025, and the game scripts were often favorable with the Hoosiers building leads. NFL defenses will present a dramatically different challenge.
strengths: The national championship context validates the production. Earning the lead role on the best team in the country is a signal that coaches trusted his ability over alternatives — that trust is earned, not given.

The 28-214-2TD receiving line gives him a PPR floor that most late-round backs lack. He can catch the ball out of the backfield, which means he'll stay on the field for some third downs and contribute in the passing game.
The 5.6 YPC shows he was efficient, not just a volume beneficiary. He's finding the hole and hitting it with enough decisiveness to produce behind a good offensive line.
weaknesses: The one-year breakout from a JMU transfer is a limited sample. The Indiana offensive line was excellent and the game scripts were favorable — his production may not translate to a less favorable NFL environment.
The 251-yard backup season in 2024 raises questions about whether the 2025 breakout is sustainable or a one-year spike driven by opportunity.
The Indiana system created favorable blocking angles and game scripts that inflated running back production. NFL defenses will present a dramatically different challenge.
black.nflFit:
Committee back with receiving upside in a zone scheme. Best as a change-of-pace option who can contribute on third downs and in the passing game.

The receiving ability is the key to his NFL role. If he can develop from 28 catches to 30-35 at the NFL level, he'll stay on the field for some third downs, which increases his snap count and his PPR value.

The zone-scheme fit matters because his efficiency at Indiana was driven by outside-zone concepts that allowed him to use his vision and patience.
black.comp:
Jaylen Warren — an undrafted back who earned a committee role through production and receiving ability. Like Warren, Black's value is in his versatility and his ability to contribute in multiple phases.

The Warren comparison is about the earn-your-role profile. Warren earned his NFL role through preseason production and receiving ability, not through draft capital. Black will need to follow a similar path.
black.dynastyOutlook:
RB4/5 with weekly RB3 upside if he earns a role. The national championship context and receiving ability are the positives; the one-year sample is the concern.

The PPR premium matters. In standard scoring, Black is a TD-dependent dart throw. In PPR, the 28 catches give him a floor that elevates him to a usable flex option.

The range of outcomes is wide. Best case: he earns a committee role and produces 700 rushing yards with 30 catches and 8 TDs — an RB3 in PPR. Worst case: the one-year sample was a system peak and he can't earn NFL snaps.
black.badgersFit:
Black is a fascinating late-round RB whose national championship production demands attention. 1,040 yards and 10 TDs on the best team in the country is no fluke — coaches trusted him over the alternatives. At pick 5.90 he's excellent value. The 28 catches add PPR appeal most RBs in this range lack. Draft him as your RB3/4 and hope the receiving ability translates.

The Indiana context is the strongest signal. He wasn't a big-name recruit — he transferred from JMU and earned the starting job on a championship team through performance.

The one-year sample is the risk, but at pick 5.90 the cost is low enough to absorb the downside.
black.verdict: Championship-tested RB with receiving upside. Intriguing late-round dart.

bell-skyler.shortReport:
A Wisconsin-to-UConn transfer whose two-year production (54-698-6TD in 2024, 52-717-5TD in 2025) shows he can produce consistently at the P5-to-G5 level. Bell's game is built on reliable hands, clean intermediate routes, and the kind of steady production that makes him a quarterback's security blanket. He's not explosive and he'll never be a WR1, but the consistency across two seasons gives him a PPR floor that most receivers in this tier can't match.

The P5-to-G5 transition is both a strength and a limitation. He proved at Wisconsin that he could compete at the Power 5 level, and his production at UConn shows he can dominate against lesser competition. The question is whether the Wisconsin tape or the UConn tape is more predictive of his NFL projection. If the Wisconsin athleticism translates, he's a solid WR4 in PPR. If the UConn production represents his ceiling against weak defenses, he's a camp body.

The consistency is the calling card. Fifty-two to 54 catches with 5-6 TDs across two seasons at UConn is remarkably steady production that suggests a reliable player rather than a hot-streak beneficiary. In dynasty, that consistency has compounding value — he'll give you 5-60 every week without the zero-catch disasters that boom/bust receivers produce.
strengths: The two-year consistency (54-698-6 and 52-717-5) is remarkable for a player in this tier. Most receivers in this range have one good season — Bell has two identical ones, which suggests sustainable production.

The reliable hands and clean route-running give him a PPR floor. He catches everything in his frame and runs precise intermediate routes that create just enough separation for his quarterback to find him.
The Wisconsin pedigree shows he was recruited by a Power 5 program and competed against Big Ten defenses before transferring. That experience gives him a higher floor than most G5 receivers.
weaknesses: The G5 competition at UConn inflates his numbers. AAC defenses aren't NFL-caliber, and his production came against coverage units that couldn't match his physical profile.
He's not explosive — no deep speed, no YAC ability, no game-breaking traits. He wins through consistency and technique, which caps his ceiling at WR4 in PPR and WR5 in standard.
The volume plateau (52-54 catches) suggests he's already near his ceiling rather than an ascending talent.
bell-skyler.nflFit:
Depth possession receiver. Best as a WR5/6 on an NFL roster who provides reliable hands and intermediate route-running. He'll earn a roster spot through consistency rather than playmaking.

The P5 pedigree gives him a higher floor than most G5 receivers. He competed at Wisconsin before transferring, which means he's faced Big Ten defenders and survived.

The lack of explosive traits caps his ceiling. He won't create his own opportunities — he needs the scheme to get him open.
bell-skyler.comp:
Russell Gage — a reliable slot receiver whose PPR value is rooted in consistent volume rather than explosive production. Like Gage, Bell will give you steady lines without the boom weeks.

The Gage comparison is about the steady-but-unspectacular profile. Gage produced 50-60 catches per season through reliability and consistency, not through elite traits. Bell has a similar profile.
bell-skyler.dynastyOutlook:
WR5/6 with PPR flex appeal. The two-year consistency is his best trait, but the G5 competition and lack of explosive traits cap his ceiling.

The PPR floor is usable. Fifty-two to 54 catches gives him a weekly baseline of 5-6 points, which is a viable WR5/6 in deep PPR formats.

The G5 inflation is the concern. If his production was driven by weak competition, his NFL numbers could decline significantly.
bell-skyler.badgersFit:
Bell is a former Badger whose consistent production at UConn (52-54 catches, 5-6 TDs per year) makes him a safe late-round WR. At pick 5.125 he's fine value as a WR5/6 with PPR floor. The Wisconsin pedigree gives him a higher floor than most G5 receivers. Don't expect fireworks — expect 5-60 every week.

The Badgers connection adds familiarity value. He knows Big Ten defenses and he proved he could compete at the P5 level before transferring.

The G5 inflation is the risk. UConn's competition isn't NFL-caliber, which means the 52-54 catches may not translate to consistent NFL production.
bell-skyler.verdict: Consistent G5 producer with P5 pedigree. Safe PPR floor play.

johnson-emmett.shortReport:
The most productive running back in the 2025 class by total yardage, and it's not close. Johnson's 251-1451-12TD rushing line combined with 46-370-3TD receiving gives him 1,821 total yards from scrimmage — the most in college football. He wasn't just a volume beneficiary; he led the nation in scrimmage yards per game, earned first-team All-American honors, and did it all in his first year as the featured back at Nebraska after a modest 2024 (124-627-5TD). The breakout is dramatic and the production is undeniable.

What makes Johnson a legitimate dynasty asset is the receiving dimension. Forty-six catches out of the backfield is elite for a college running back — it's the kind of volume that projects to a three-down role at the NFL level. He's not just catching dump-offs; he's running angle routes, swing screens, and wheel concepts that suggest genuine route-running ability from the backfield. In PPR formats, that receiving floor elevates him from a standard grinder to a flex option with RB2 upside every week.

The one-year breakout is the risk. Going from 124 carries and 627 yards in 2024 to 251 carries and 1,451 yards in 2025 is a massive leap that could represent genuine development or a system opportunity that aligned perfectly. Nebraska's offensive line improved significantly in 2025, and the game scripts were often favorable. The NFL will present a different challenge — smaller holes, faster linebackers, and defenses that won't be caught off guard.
strengths: The 1,821 total yards from scrimmage led the nation and earned first-team All-American honors. That volume proves he can handle a full workload and produce at the highest level of college football.

The 46-370-3TD receiving line is elite for a running back and projects to a three-down role. He's running real routes, not just catching check-downs — that's the kind of receiving ability that keeps backs on the field in the NFL.
The 5.8 YPC on 251 carries shows he was efficient even with heavy volume. He's not just a grinder who gets what's blocked — he's creating extra yards with vision and decisiveness.
The first-team AA honor validates the production as elite, not just volume-driven.
weaknesses: The one-year breakout from a modest 2024 (124-627-5) introduces the classic one-year-wonder risk. The leap is dramatic enough to be either a genuine development signal or a system peak.
Nebraska's improved offensive line in 2025 may have been the primary driver of his efficiency. If the blocking created the holes, his NFL efficiency could decline behind an average line.
The 251 carries in a single season is heavy mileage for a one-year starter, which raises questions about his long-term durability.
johnson-emmett.nflFit:
Three-down back in a zone scheme. The receiving ability makes him a legitimate three-down option who can stay on the field in all situations. Best in a team that values pass-catching backs.

The receiving dimension is the separator. Forty-six catches project to 35-40 at the NFL level in a committee, which gives him a PPR floor that most running backs in this tier can't match.

The zone-scheme fit matters because his efficiency at Nebraska was driven by outside-zone concepts that maximized his vision and decisiveness.
johnson-emmett.comp:
Josh Jacobs — a productive workhorse whose receiving ability gives him PPR relevance beyond his rushing production. Like Jacobs, Johnson's value is in his total production from scrimmage rather than any single elite trait.

The Jacobs comparison is about the volume-plus-receiving profile. Jacobs has been a productive fantasy back because he contributes both as a rusher and as a receiver. Johnson projects to a similar arc if he lands in the right system.
johnson-emmett.dynastyOutlook:
RB3 with weekly RB2 upside. The total production is elite but the one-year sample introduces risk. In PPR, the 46 catches give him a floor that most RBs in this tier can't match.

The PPR premium is significant. In standard scoring, Johnson is a TD-dependent grinder. In PPR, the 46 catches add 4-5 points per week that elevate him to a usable flex option.

The one-year breakout risk is real but the All-American validation partially mitigates it.
johnson-emmett.badgersFit:
Johnson is the best late-round RB in this class for PPR. 1,821 total yards and a first-team AA is elite production by any measure. The 46 catches are the fantasy hook — that receiving ability projects to a three-down role that most RBs in this range can't offer. At pick 5.161 he's a steal. Draft him as your RB2/3 and enjoy the PPR floor.

The receiving dimension is the separator. In a league where PPR points matter, a running back who catches 35-40 balls is worth more than a pure rusher with higher YPC.

The one-year breakout is the risk, but at pick 5.161 the cost is low enough to absorb the downside. The All-American honor validates the production.
johnson-emmett.verdict: All-American workhorse with elite receiving. Best late-round PPR RB.

allen-kaytron.shortReport:
Nicholas Singleton's backfield partner for four years at Penn State, Allen finally got his chance as the lead back in 2025 and didn't disappoint — 1,303 rushing yards and 15 touchdowns, with 22-152-1TD receiving, earned him third-team All-American honors. The production is impressive, but the context matters: Allen was splitting carries with Singleton for three full years before the 2025 breakout, and his efficiency metrics (5.7 YPC in 2024, though the 2025 split is muddied) suggest a solid but unspectacular runner.

The 15 rushing touchdowns are the fantasy hook and they're rooted in a real skill — goal-line patience and vision. Allen reads the blocking scheme near the end zone, finds the soft shoulder, and squeezes through with the kind of short-yardage instinct that translates to any level. The TD production has been consistent throughout his career (10→6→8→15), and the 2025 spike reflects his expanded role as the lead back rather than a sudden improvement in his finishing ability.

The receiving production (22-152-1TD) is modest but functional — he's not a natural pass-catcher, but he can handle check-downs and screen passes without drops. The limitation is that he's never going to be a 40-catch back, which caps his PPR floor. In standard scoring, the 15 TDs make him a solid RB2 option. In PPR, the limited receiving volume drops him to RB3/4 territory.
strengths: The 15 rushing touchdowns in 2025 reflect genuine goal-line ability that translates to the NFL. He's been scoring touchdowns consistently throughout his career (10→6→8→15), which shows the skill is real and sustainable.
The 1,303 rushing yards as the featured back prove he can handle a full workload. After splitting carries for three years, he showed he can produce when given the opportunity.
The four-year Penn State pedigree means he's experienced and NFL-ready. He's been in a pro-style program with elite coaching and has faced the best defenses in the Big Ten.
The third-team All-American honor validates the 2025 production as genuine, not just a volume spike.
weaknesses: The split-backfield role for three years raises questions about why Penn State never gave him the lead role earlier. Was it because Singleton was better, or because Allen couldn't handle a full workload? NFL teams will wonder the same thing.
The receiving production (22-152-1TD) is too modest for a three-down profile. He'll come off the field on third-and-long in the NFL, which caps his snap count and his PPR value.
The career mileage is substantial — four years of Power 5 production means he enters the NFL with significant wear on his body.
The efficiency is good but not elite. He's a steady 5.5-5.7 YPC back who gets what's blocked rather than creating explosive plays.
allen-kaytron.nflFit:
Early-down grinder with goal-line value in a power scheme. Best as a committee back who handles first and second down and gets the ball near the end zone. He's not a three-down back due to receiving limitations.

The goal-line role is his NFL floor. Allen's patience and vision near the end zone will earn him short-yardage carries regardless of his overall snap count. That role guarantees him TD-dependent fantasy value.

The receiving limitation caps his ceiling. With only 22 catches in his best season, he projects to 15-20 at the NFL level, which isn't enough for PPR relevance.
allen-kaytron.comp:
Damien Harris — a reliable early-down grinder with goal-line ability whose fantasy value is concentrated in TD-heavy formats. Like Harris, Allen will produce through volume and TDs rather than through receiving or explosive plays.

The Harris comparison is about the two-down grinder profile. Harris was a productive fantasy back in New England because he got volume and scored TDs, but his PPR value was limited by his receiving production. Allen projects to a similar arc.
allen-kaytron.dynastyOutlook:
RB3/4 with weekly RB2 upside in TD-friendly spots. The 15 TDs are the ceiling driven by an expanded role; expect 8-10 in the NFL. In standard, the TDs make him a usable flex. In PPR, the 22 catches limit his floor.

The TD regression is coming. Fifteen TDs in a featured role at Penn State won't repeat in an NFL committee. Expect 8-10 TDs as a realistic projection.

The PPR penalty is real. With only 22 catches, Allen provides almost no receiving floor. In PPR, he's an RB4 unless he scores. In standard or TD-heavy formats, his volume and TDs make him a usable flex.
allen-kaytron.badgersFit:
Allen is a TD-driven RB whose 15 rushing TDs in 2025 are enticing, but the receiving (22 catches) caps his PPR value. At pick 5.187 he's solid value as your RB3 in standard scoring. The Penn State pedigree and four-year production give him a higher floor than most backs in this range. In PPR, the limited receiving drops his value. Draft him for TDs, not for catches.

The goal-line ability is the real skill. He's scored 39 rushing TDs in four years at Penn State — that's not a fluke, it's a translatable NFL skill.

The receiving limitation is the PPR concern. With only 22 catches, he comes off the field on third-and-long, which caps his snap count and his PPR floor.
allen-kaytron.verdict: TD-driven Penn State workhorse. Standard-scoring RB3 with limited PPR appeal.

claiborne.shortReport:
A two-year workhorse at Wake Forest whose 21 touchdowns across 2024-25 (11 and 10) prove he can score consistently even in a middling ACC offense. Claiborne's game is built on power and patience — he's not going to make defenders miss in the open field, but he's going to find the soft shoulder near the goal line and squeeze through with the kind of finishing ability that translates to short-yardage situations at any level. The 5.1 YPC in 2025 (up from 4.6 in 2024) shows he's improving even as defenses key on him.

The two-year production is the strongest signal in his profile. Unlike one-year breakouts who could be riding a system peak, Claiborne produced double-digit touchdowns in back-to-back seasons at a program that doesn't get much offensive line help. That consistency suggests the TD production is player-driven — he's creating his own scoring opportunities through vision and patience rather than riding elite blocking.

The receiving development (14-98-0TD in 2024 to 19-131-1TD in 2025) is modest but trending in the right direction. He's not going to be a 40-catch back, but if he can develop into a 25-catch option, he'll have enough PPR floor to be a usable flex play. Without that receiving growth, he's a standard-scoring-only asset whose value is entirely dependent on goal-line carries.
strengths: The 21 touchdowns in two seasons at Wake Forest prove the scoring is sustainable and player-driven, not system-driven. He scored 11 and 10 TDs in back-to-back seasons in a middling ACC offense — that's finishing ability, not scheme.
The improving efficiency (4.6→5.1 YPC) in his second year as the starter shows he's developing as a runner, not declining under heavier workloads.
The emerging receiving production (14→19 catches, 98→131 yards) suggests he's developing a pass-catching dimension that could expand his NFL role beyond two downs.
The two-year sample is more projectable than the one-year breakouts elsewhere in this tier.
weaknesses: The ACC competition is a step below the SEC and Big Ten. Wake Forest faces defenses that won't produce multiple NFL draft picks, which inflates his production relative to backs from elite conferences.

The power-first running style limits his ceiling. He doesn't make defenders miss in the open field and he doesn't have the speed to break long runs. His big-play rate is low, which caps his weekly upside.
The modest receiving production (19 catches) is still too low for a true three-down profile. He'll come off the field on third-and-long in the NFL.

The Wake Forest offensive line wasn't elite, but the system featured him heavily — his 228 carries in 2024 and 179 in 2025 show he was the focal point. That volume may not translate to an NFL committee.
claiborne.nflFit:
Early-down committee back with goal-line value. Best in a power scheme near the goal line where his patience and vision can produce short-yardage touchdowns.

The goal-line role is his NFL floor. Even in a committee, his finishing ability near the end zone will earn him short-yardage carries. That role guarantees him TD-dependent fantasy value.

The emerging receiving production gives him a path to more snaps. If he can develop from 19 catches to 25-30, he'll stay on the field for some third downs, which increases his PPR value.
claiborne.comp:
AJ Dillon — a power back with emerging receiving ability whose fantasy value is concentrated in goal-line situations and TD-heavy formats. Like Dillon, Claiborne produces through power and volume rather than explosiveness.

The Dillon comparison is about the power-back-with-emerging-receiving profile. Dillon has been productive in Green Bay because he scores TDs and contributes modestly in the passing game. Claiborne projects to a similar role.
claiborne.dynastyOutlook:
RB3/4 with weekly RB2 upside in TD-friendly spots. The two-year consistency is the strongest signal, but the ACC competition and limited receiving cap his ceiling.

The two-year TD consistency is the differentiator. Eleven and 10 TDs in back-to-back seasons at Wake Forest is more reliable than a one-year spike, which gives Claiborne a higher floor than most backs in this tier.

The PPR penalty is moderate. With 19 catches, he provides some receiving value but not enough for a three-down profile. In PPR, he's an RB4 with TD upside. In standard, he's an RB3.
claiborne.badgersFit:
Claiborne is a two-year TD producer whose 21 TDs at Wake Forest are the most consistent scoring from any RB in this tier. At pick 5.198 he's excellent value — the two-year sample is more reliable than the one-year breakouts elsewhere. The 19 catches add a tiny PPR floor. Draft him as your RB3 in standard scoring and enjoy the TD floor.

The two-year consistency is the key selling point. Back-to-back double-digit TD seasons at a middling ACC program is player-driven production.

The ACC competition is the discount factor. Wake Forest doesn't face NFL-caliber defenses consistently, which means his production may be slightly inflated.
claiborne.verdict: Two-year TD machine at Wake Forest. Reliable standard-scoring RB3.

williams-antonio.shortReport:
A slot/returner whose massive 2024 breakout (75-904-11TD) was followed by a sharp regression in 2025 (55-604-4TD) that raises serious questions about which version is the real Antonio Williams. The TD decline from 11 to 4 is the most alarming trend — combined with the catch drop from 75 to 55 and the yardage collapse from 904 to 604, it suggests the 2024 season was an opportunity-and-scheme peak rather than a sustainable skill level. Williams was the hot hand in Clemson's offense in 2024; by 2025, defenses adjusted and the targets distributed more evenly.

The slot route-running that produced the 2024 breakout is a real skill, even if the numbers regressed. Williams runs option routes, angle routes, and crossers with the kind of quickness and timing that create separation against man coverage. Those are translatable NFL skills that don't disappear just because the TD rate declined. The issue is whether those skills produce WR3 numbers (the 2024 version) or WR6 numbers (the 2025 version) at the NFL level. The honest answer is probably somewhere in between — expect 55-65 catches with 5-7 TDs as a realistic projection.

The punt-return ability (10-89 in 2024, 8-61 in 2025) gives him a roster floor that most slot receivers lack. Even if the offense doesn't feature him as a primary target, he'll contribute on special teams, which means he'll be active on game days and have occasional fantasy relevance. In return-yard leagues, that contribution adds 2-3 points per week. But in standard formats, the 2025 regression is the number you should draft against, not the 2024 breakout.
strengths: The 2024 breakout proved he can produce at a high level when the offense features him. Seventy-five catches and 11 touchdowns in a single season is elite production regardless of context. The slot route-running that produced those numbers — option routes, angle routes, crossers — is a real skill that translates to the NFL.
The punt-return ability gives him a roster floor that most slot receivers lack. Even if the offense doesn't feature him, he'll contribute on special teams.
weaknesses: The 2025 regression (75→55 catches, 11→4 TDs) is the defining concern. That decline reflects a normalization of his role in the offense. In the NFL, he won't be the focal point of any red-zone offense, which means the 4-TD version is closer to his floor than the 11-TD version.
The declining YPR (17.6→12.1→11.0) shows he's becoming less efficient as his volume increases. That's a natural trend for slot receivers but it means his per-catch value is declining.
williams-antonio.nflFit:
Slot/returner in a spread offense. Best with a quick-release quarterback who uses the slot as a chain-mover. The return value gives him a roster floor.

The return value is roster-floor insurance. Even if the offense doesn't feature Williams as a receiver, he'll contribute on special teams.

The slot route-running is his receiving skill but his physical limitations — modest size and no deep speed — cap his receiving ceiling at WR5/6.
williams-antonio.comp: Jamison Crowder — a slot/returner whose PPR value fluctuates with his role in the offense. Like Crowder, Williams will have productive weeks when the game script favors slot targets and quiet weeks when it doesn't.
williams-antonio.dynastyOutlook:
WR5/6 with the 2024 breakout as his ceiling and the 2025 regression as his floor. Draft against the 2025 numbers, not the 2024 ones.

The weekly profile will be inconsistent. Williams will give you 5-60-0 most weeks with occasional TD weeks that push him to WR3 territory. That inconsistency makes him a bench stash rather than a starter.

The return value adds 2-3 PPR per week in return-yard leagues, which elevates him to a viable WR4/5.
williams-antonio.badgersFit:
Williams is a regression candidate whose 2024 breakout (75-904-11TD) won't repeat. Draft against the 2025 numbers (55-604-4TD) which are more predictive of his NFL production. At pick 4.71 he's a fine bench WR in PPR and return-yard leagues. Don't reach based on the 2024 stats.

The Badgers-specific concern is the regression. The 2024 numbers were an opportunity peak, not a sustainable skill level.

The return value is the saving grace in return-yard formats. In standard PPR, he's a WR6 whose value depends on occasional TD weeks.
williams-antonio.verdict: Regressing slot/returner. Draft the 2025 version, not the 2024 version.

thompson.shortReport:
The most explosive one-year breakout receiver in this class, and it came at his third program. Thompson transferred from Texas to Oklahoma to Mississippi State, and the Bulldogs' offense finally unlocked his deep speed — 57-1054-6TD with an 18.5 YPR that led the SEC in receiving yards. The production is undeniable, but the context raises legitimate questions: why couldn't he produce at Texas or Oklahoma, two of the most receiver-friendly programs in college football?

The two-transfer arc is the most important contextual factor in his evaluation. At Texas, Thompson was a highly-rated recruit who couldn't crack the rotation. At Oklahoma, he caught just 19 balls in 2024 for 230 yards — modest numbers for a player with his athletic profile. At Mississippi State, he exploded for 1,054 yards in a single season. The optimistic interpretation is that he finally found the right system and quarterback to unlock his talent. The pessimistic interpretation is that he dominated weaker SEC West defenses in a system that schemed him open, and his production is more about Mississippi State's passing attack than his individual ability.

The 18.5 YPR is the number that will get him drafted and it reflects genuine deep-ball ability. Thompson tracks the ball downfield with natural hands, adjusts to underthrown passes without losing speed, and produces explosive plays that change the scoreboard. That skill is scheme-independent — he'll catch deep balls in any offense. The question is whether he can produce at the intermediate level too, or whether he's a one-dimensional deep threat whose 57 catches represent a volume ceiling rather than a stepping stone to more.
strengths: The 18.5 YPR led the SEC and reflects genuine deep-ball ability. He tracks the ball downfield with natural hands and adjusts to underthrown passes without losing speed.
The 57 catches prove he can handle volume, not just deep shots. Most deep-ball specialists plateau at 30-40 catches — Thompson got to 57, which suggests real route-running development.
The 1,054 yards broke the Mississippi State single-season receiving record, proving he can be the focal point of an offense.
weaknesses: The two-transfer failure before the breakout is a major red flag. He couldn't produce at Texas or Oklahoma — two elite programs with excellent quarterback play. The Mississippi State breakout could be real development or it could be a system/competition peak.
The one-year sample at Mississippi State is too small to project with confidence. He could be an ascending talent who needed the right fit, or he could be a one-year wonder riding a favorable system.
The SEC West defensive competition he faced at Mississippi State was uneven. Some of his biggest games came against weaker defenses.
thompson.nflFit:
Deep-ball Z-receiver in a vertical offense. The speed and ball-tracking are translatable but the route tree needs to expand.

The deep-ball tracking is his most translatable skill. He adjusts to the ball in flight with natural hands and body control, which means he'll catch deep balls even when the throw isn't perfect.

The two-transfer arc is concerning for NFL teams who want reliable producers. The inability to produce at Texas and Oklahoma introduces risk that the Mississippi State breakout was system-dependent.
thompson.comp:
DeSean Jackson — deep-ball speed with boom/bust production. Like Jackson, Thompson will win weeks with 80-yard touchdowns and disappear in weeks where the deep ball doesn't connect.

The Jackson comparison is about the deep-speed profile. Jackson's fantasy value was concentrated in big-play weeks, and Thompson projects to a similar arc.
thompson.dynastyOutlook:
WR4/5 with boom weeks. The 18.5 YPR is tantalizing but the two-transfer history and one-year sample introduce significant risk.

The range of outcomes is wide. Best case: the Mississippi State breakout was real and he produces 50-800-6 in the NFL. Worst case: the breakout was system-dependent and he can't earn targets at the NFL level.

The deep-ball ability is the floor. Even if he can't produce volume, he'll catch deep balls when targeted, which gives him occasional boom weeks.
thompson.badgersFit:
Thompson is the most exciting boom/bust WR in this tier. 1,054 yards and 18.5 YPR at Mississippi State is electric, but the two-transfer failure (Texas → Oklahoma → MSU) is a red flag. At pick 5.105 he's worth the gamble — if the breakout is real, you've got a WR3 with deep-ball upside. If it's not, you've lost a fifth-round pick.

The deep speed is the hook. In any offense that takes vertical shots, Thompson will be a threat.

The two-transfer history is the risk. If he couldn't produce at Texas or Oklahoma, the MSU breakout might be a system peak.
thompson.verdict: Explosive one-year breakout with deep speed. High-variance boom/bust WR.

wetjen.shortReport:
The most unique fantasy profile in this entire draft class — a walk-on Iowa receiver whose 20-151-1TD receiving line is laughably modest, but whose 4 return touchdowns (3 punt returns, 1 kick return) in 2025 earned him the Jet Award as college football's top return specialist. Wetjen's entire fantasy value is concentrated in return-yard leagues where those return touchdowns and 500+ return yards translate to real points. In standard formats, he's completely unrosterable.

The return ability is legitimate and it's rooted in specific skills that translate to the NFL: vision to identify the lane, burst to hit the gap, and elusiveness to make the first defender miss in space. Four return touchdowns in a single season is elite at any level — most NFL return specialists average 0-1 per year. Even with regression to 1-2 return TDs per NFL season, Wetjen provides a dimension of scoring that no other player in this class offers.

The receiving limitation is absolute and non-negotiable. Twenty catches for 151 yards at Iowa — one of the least productive passing offenses in college football — doesn't project to any fantasy receiving value. He's not going to develop into a 50-catch slot receiver at the NFL level because the skills aren't there. His value begins and ends with special teams, which means he's only relevant in leagues that score return yards and return touchdowns.
strengths: The 4 return touchdowns in 2025 are historically productive. He earned the Jet Award as college football's top return specialist, which validates the skill as real and not fluky.
The return skills — vision, burst, elusiveness — are translatable to the NFL and will earn him a roster spot regardless of his receiving production.
weaknesses: The 20-151-1TD receiving line is negligible and doesn't project to any fantasy receiving value. He's a return specialist, not a receiver.
Return touchdowns regress heavily at the NFL level. Four per year will become 0-1, which dramatically reduces his fantasy value even in return-yard leagues.
wetjen.nflFit:
Return specialist. He'll earn a roster spot through punt and kick returns but won't contribute as a receiver.

The return ability is his entire NFL value. Even if the offense never targets him, he'll stick on a roster because return specialists are valuable on special teams.

The receiving ceiling is nonexistent. Twenty catches at Iowa doesn't translate to any NFL receiving role.
wetjen.comp:
Mecole Hardman early career — a return specialist with minimal receiving value whose fantasy relevance is limited to return-yard leagues.

The Hardman comparison is about the return-first profile. Hardman's early NFL value was as a returner, not a receiver. Wetjen projects to a similar role.
wetjen.dynastyOutlook:
WR7 in standard. WR4/5 in return-yard leagues. His value is 100% format-dependent.

In return-yard leagues, the 4 return TDs project to 1-2 at the NFL level plus 500+ return yards, which adds 5-8 points per week. That's legitimate fantasy value.

In standard leagues, he's unrosterable. Twenty catches doesn't project to any receiving production.
wetjen.badgersFit:

young.shortReport:
A big-bodied receiver whose talent has never matched his production due to a combination of injuries, transfers, and off-field concerns. Young's career spans four seasons across Miami and Georgia, producing 116 catches total — a number that should be much higher given his physical tools. His 2025 season at Georgia was cut short by a broken ankle against Ole Miss, limiting him to just 20-261-1TD before the injury ended his year. The talent is evident on tape, but the reliability is nonexistent.

The injury history is the defining constraint on his dynasty value. The broken ankle in 2025 is just the latest in a series of setbacks that have prevented Young from stringing together a full, healthy season. In dynasty, availability is the most important ability, and Young has proven he can't be counted on to play 17 games. Even when healthy, his production has been modest — 13-128-2TD in 2024 at Georgia is underwhelming for a receiver with his physical profile playing in one of the best offenses in college football.

The Georgia context should have elevated his production, and the fact that it didn't tells you something. Playing alongside elite talent in a program that develops NFL receivers, Young still couldn't earn consistent targets. Whether that's due to injuries limiting his practice time, off-field concerns affecting his standing with coaches, or simply a gap between his physical tools and his football skills, the result is the same: he's a player whose ceiling is much higher than his floor, and the floor has been his reality far more often than the ceiling.
strengths: The physical tools are legitimate — size, speed, and body control that create mismatches against most defensive backs. When healthy and engaged, he can win on contested catches and deep routes.
The Georgia pedigree means he's been coached at an elite level with NFL-caliber preparation.
weaknesses: The injury history (broken ankle in 2025, various other setbacks) makes him an availability risk that dynasty managers can't rely on.

The modest production despite elite surroundings — 13-128-2TD in 2024 at Georgia, 20-261-1TD in 2025 before the ankle — suggests the physical tools haven't translated to consistent football production.
Off-field concerns have affected his standing at multiple programs, adding another layer of risk to an already risky profile.
young.nflFit:
Developmental X-receiver. Best on a roster that can afford to stash him while he recovers from the ankle and develops his game.

The physical tools give him a path to an NFL role if he can stay healthy and avoid off-field issues. The Georgia coaching has prepared him for NFL concepts.

The availability risk is the NFL concern. Teams want reliable players who can practice and play consistently.
young.comp:
Kelvin Harmon — physical tools without consistent production. Like Harmon, Young's value is in his potential rather than his track record.

The Harmon comparison is about the tools-over-production profile. Harmon had the physical profile to be a productive NFL receiver but couldn't stay on the field consistently. Young has a similar arc.
young.dynastyOutlook:
WR6/7 with upside if he can stay healthy. The physical tools are tantalizing but the injury history and modest production cap his value.

The range of outcomes is wide. Best case: he recovers from the ankle, stays healthy, and produces 40-500-4 in a defined role. Worst case: the injuries continue and he's out of the league within two years.

The off-field concerns add another layer of risk that makes him a taxi-squad-only stash.

If he doesn't, you've lost nothing.
